Key verse: Num 30:5 — father can annul a daughter's vow "in the day that he heareth." Num 30:2 binds a man's own vow.

Interpretive implication: Num 30 does not govern Jephthah's case directly (he is the vower, not the annuller of a daughter's vow). BUT Num 30 demonstrates Torah's willingness to annul vows to protect vulnerable parties — a principle that supports the existence of off-ramps (cross-reference Lev 5:4-5). Jephthah did not avail himself of any.

docs.title: 1 Samuel 1:11 (Hannah's controlling parallel)
docs.text:
Key verse: unetattiw laYHWH kol yemei chayyav umorah lo yaaleh al-rosho` ("then I will give him unto the LORD all the days of his life, and no razor shall come up on his head").

Lexical contrast: - Hannah: natan laYHWH kol yemei chayyav + Nazirite marker - Jephthah: hayah laYHWH + Hiphil alah + olah

Interpretive implication: Hannah's vow is the paradigmatic lifelong-dedication formula. Jephthah DOES NOT use it. The absence of natan, of kol yemei chayyav, and of any Nazirite/separation language means Jephthah is not speaking the language of dedication. If he had intended dedication, the template was famous, available, and culturally canonical. He used a different template — and every other instance of his template reports actual burnt offering.

docs.title: 1 Samuel 1:22-28 (Samuel's fulfillment)
docs.text:
Context: Hannah weans Samuel; brings him to Shiloh; leaves him to minister before Eli. Samuel LIVES and SERVES. He is lent (sha'ul) to YHWH for life.

Interpretive implication: The text shows us what lifelong dedication looks like in practice: the dedicated person lives, grows up at the sanctuary, ministers before the priest, wears the ephod (1 Sam 2:18), and becomes a prophet. There is ZERO narrative trace of any such career for Jephthah's daughter. The annual four-day rite of Jdg 11:40 is not "pilgrims visit Jephthah's daughter at Shiloh" — it is women going out to the mountains to lament/commemorate her. This is a critical narrative asymmetry between Samuel's dedication and Jephthah's daughter's fate.

docs.title: 1 Samuel 2:22; Exodus 38:8 (Women at the tabernacle)
docs.text:
Key verses: Women "assembling at the door of the tabernacle" (both verses use the feminine participle hatsov'ot).

Interpretive implication: These verses demonstrate that some women served in tabernacle-adjacent roles. But neither text describes a vowed celibate institution; Num 30:9 acknowledges vowed women could be divorced or widowed. There is no OT Mosaic category of "perpetual virgin dedicated to the sanctuary." Henry (9412) is right: the OT contains no law, custom, or usage establishing celibacy-as-religion. The dedication reading of Jephthah's daughter requires inventing a category not otherwise attested in the Hebrew Bible.

docs.title: Genesis 22:1-14 (Akedah — arrested sacrifice)
docs.text:
Key elements: et-binkha et-yechidkha (v.2); wehaalehu sham leolah` (v.2); Abraham builds altar, binds Isaac, takes knife (v.10); YHWH intervenes, ram substitutes (vv.12-13).

Interpretive implication: The Akedah provides the lexical and thematic template Jdg 11 visibly echoes — yachid, healah+olah`. But the Akedah is arrested by divine intervention. The analogous expectation for Jdg 11 would be an arrest. No arrest is recorded. The narrator does not say "and YHWH called from heaven... lay not thine hand upon the maid." The narrator's silence on arrest is itself evidence the arrest did not occur. Gen 22 is the template; Jdg 11 is the template un-arrested — the tragic inverse of Gen 22, where a father who is NOT commanded by God nonetheless pushes through to the knife where Abraham was stopped.

docs.title: Genesis 22:2 vs Jdg 11:31 (Hiphil alah + olah collocation)
docs.text:
Exact parallel: Gen 22:2 wehaalehu sham leolah; Jdg 11:31wehaalitihuolah`. Both command/promise the action with identical cultic vocabulary. Gen 22 carries through to raised knife (v.10) before arrest; Jdg 11 is told to us at the utterance and echoed at the fulfillment.

Interpretive implication: The vocabulary is cultic-technical and refers to actual burnt-offering presentation. If Gen 22 used this vocabulary for dedication-not-sacrifice, we would expect the narrator's emphasis to fall on life-service, not on the knife. It falls on the knife. The same vocabulary in Jdg 11 therefore does not carry a latent dedication-sense.

docs.title: Hebrews 11:32-34 (Faith roll)
docs.text:
Greek parsing: Nine aorist verbs in vv.33-34, all in the military-deliverance semantic field: katagonizomai (subdued), ergazomai (wrought righteousness), epitugchano (obtained promises), phrasso (stopped mouths), sbennymi (quenched fire), pheugo (escaped sword), dynamoo (strengthened from weakness), ginomai ischuroi (became mighty in war), klino (turned armies to flight).

Interpretive implication: NONE of these aorists describes a priestly/cultic act, a vow-execution, or a sacrifice. Each maps to a military-deliverance moment in the lives of the listed figures. For Jephthah: katagonizomai and klino map to Jdg 11:29, 32-33 (Ammonite campaign). The vow-execution of Jdg 11:39 is NOT part of what Heb 11 commends.

Heb 11 notoriously commends discrete faith-acts, not whole biographies: - Samson included, but Heb 11 does not endorse his Philistine wife (Jdg 14:1-3) or his visit to Gaza's harlot (16:1). - David included (not by name in 11:32's list but in v.32 as "David"), but Heb 11 does not endorse his adultery/murder. - Gideon included, but Heb 11 does not endorse his gold-ephod idolatry (Jdg 8:27). - Rahab commended for hiding spies (v.31), not for her prior trade.

Verdict: Heb 11:32 is symmetrical on the vow question. Commending Jephthah's faith-victory over Ammon is not an endorsement of his vow or its execution. The v1 conclusion correctly held this line; the v2 conclusion agrees.

docs.title: Leviticus 18:21; 20:2-5 (Molech prohibition)
docs.text: Interpretive implication: The Pentateuch explicitly names human-child-to-Molech as a capital offense. The language targets Molech specifically but the act — "passing seed through fire" — is the abomination, independent of the god to whom it is offered. Deut 12:31 generalizes: "Thou shalt NOT DO SO unto the LORD thy God" — applying Canaanite child-immolation to YHWH-worship is categorically forbidden. Jephthah's act (on the sacrifice reading) violates this categorical prohibition, regardless of his intent to "honor" YHWH.

docs.title: Deuteronomy 12:29-31; 18:9-12 (Categorical prohibition)
docs.text: Interpretive implication: These are general-principle texts — no private, vow-based, or individually-motivated exception is contemplated. The prohibition is absolute.

docs.title: Jeremiah 7:31 (Divine disavowal — CENTRAL)
docs.text:
Hebrew parsing: asher lo-tsivviti weloalta al-libbi — "which I commanded not, neither came it into my heart."

Interpretive implication: YHWH's own disavowal of child-immolation is categorical. If Jdg 11:39 is literal sacrifice, it was NOT divinely commanded, NOT divinely accepted, and NOT divinely wanted. Jer 7:31 forecloses the reading in which YHWH "accepted" Jephthah's daughter as a pleasing burnt offering. (Note: this does NOT foreclose the literal-sacrifice reading itself — it only forecloses any interpretation in which the act was divinely sanctioned. Josephus' own verdict — "neither conformable to the law nor acceptable to God" — fits this framing exactly.)

This is where the literal-sacrifice reading's theological cost shrinks. The reading does NOT require God to have approved the act; it only requires the narrator to have reported a real, sinful, Torah-violating act of a flawed judge in a dark era. That is exactly what Judges-as-a-book is for.

docs.title: Jeremiah 19:5; 32:35; Ezekiel 16:20-21; 20:26, 31; 23:37-39; Ps 106:37-38; Isa 57:5; Mic 6:7
docs.text: Collectively: These texts denounce child-sacrifice as the paradigm abomination, explicitly classifying it as YHWH-rejected. They form the backdrop against which Jephthah's act (if literal) must be read — as continuous with, not separate from, the pattern Israel's prophets condemn.

docs.title: 2 Kings 3:27 (Mesha — explicit wayyaalehu olah)
docs.text:
Hebrew parsing: wayyiqqach et-bno habekhor... wayyaalehu olah al-hachomah`.

Interpretive implication: This is the ONLY other OT narrative where a parent offers up a child as a burnt offering. The verb is Hiphil alah + direct-object pronoun + olah — IDENTICAL construction to Jephthah's vow (Jdg 11:31). Mesha the Moabite does it on Moabite soil, to Chemosh. Mesha's act is condemned in the narrative ("there was great indignation against Israel"). The lexical identity between Jdg 11:31's vow and 2 Kgs 3:27's report is substantial negative evidence for the dedication reading: this is the exact lexical pattern used for actual human burnt offerings.

docs.title: 1 Samuel 14:24-45 (Saul's rash oath — community override)
docs.text:
Interpretive implication: The narrative pattern demonstrates that rash oaths CAN be overridden. Saul's oath forbids eating; Jonathan eats; Saul condemns Jonathan to death; the people intervene: "Shall Jonathan die, who hath wrought this great salvation in Israel? God forbid!" (14:45). Jonathan is saved.

Jephthah's narrative has no parallel community intervention. This is ambiguous evidence: either (a) the community morally collapsed in the Judges-era (consistent with Jdg 17-21's refrain) and let the sacrifice happen, or (b) there was no crisis requiring override because dedication (not death) was the resolution. Reading (a) fits better with Judges' overall theological arc: the book's refrain "every man did that which was right in his own eyes" pervasively condemns the moral state of the era.

docs.title: Ecclesiastes 5:4-6 (Vow ethic + error clause)
docs.text: Interpretive implication: Qohelet acknowledges that rash vows can be mitigated — "neither say thou before the angel, that it was an error" (5:6). An "error" confession is possible but limited; Lev 5:4-5's atonement pathway is the cultic instantiation. Jephthah ignored all off-ramps.

docs.title: Genesis 24:16 (Rebekah — bethulah we'ish lo yedaah`)
docs.text: Interpretive implication: A WX-QATAL parallel clause describing a living, unmarried woman about to be married. Grammatically parallel to Jdg 11:39b. Rebekah's clause is circumstantial-present-state, not durative-future-lifetime-celibacy (she marries within days). This confirms the grammar-library verdict that WX-QATAL is state-at-moment, not subsequent-lifetime.

docs.title: 1 Kings 1:4 (Abishag — wehamelek lo yedaah`)
docs.text: Interpretive implication: Another WX-QATAL parallel, inverted subject (masc.). Abishag IS LIVING; but the clause describes David's state-at-the-moment, not Abishag's lifetime chastity. In fact, Abishag later features in Solomon's succession narrative (1 Kgs 2:17-22). WX-QATAL is state-at-moment, not lifetime claim.

docs.title: Numbers 21:2 (Israel's Hormah vow)
docs.text:
Key verse: im-naton titten et-haam hazzeh beyadi wehacharamti et-areyhem.

Interpretive implication: Structurally parallel to Jephthah's vow (im + naton titten + weqatal apodosis). BUT the apodosis uses cherem, not olah. Israel's Hormah vow is a lawful cherem-ban against an enemy's cities. Jephthah's vow is a private neder with sacrificial vocabulary. Different legal categories. Num 21 is not a precedent for Jdg 11.

docs.title: Genesis 28:20-22 (Jacob's vow)
docs.text: Interpretive implication: Jacob's vow uses wehayah YHWH li lelohim + tithe-promise. No sacrificial collocation. Not a precedent for Jephthah's language.

docs.title: 2 Samuel 15:7-8 (Absalom's vow)
docs.text: Interpretive implication: Same im-protasis / weqatal-apodosis template; apodosis is service-worship (weavadti et-YHWH`). No sacrificial collocation.

docs.title: Pattern 1: The vow-utterance vs. the vow-report use DIFFERENT lexical registers
docs.text:
Supporting verses: - Jdg 11:31 (vow-utterance): Hiphil alah + olah — SACRIFICIAL - Jdg 11:39a (vow-report): wayyaas lah et-nidro asher nadar— NEUTRAL (bareasah) - Jdg 11:39b (narrator gloss):wehi lo-yadah ish — MARITAL/SEXUAL - Jdg 11:40 (rite description): letannot — AMBIGUOUS (recount or lament)

Analysis: The register-shift from sacrificial utterance to neutral report is real, but it does NOT establish a reading change. The natural interpretation is that the narrator's neutral asah defers to the vow-text — "he did his vow" means "he did what v.31 said." Since v.31 specified a burnt offering, v.39 means he offered her as a burnt offering. The narrator's restraint is literary (writing by reference) not theological (signaling dedication).

docs.title: Pattern 2: WX-QATAL clauses are attendant-state, not durative-subsequent
docs.text:
Supporting verses: - Gen 3:1 wehanachash hayah arum — existing state at scene-open - Gen 13:2 we'Avram kaved me'od bammiqneh bakkeseph uvazzahav — existing state - Gen 24:16 bethulah we'ish lo yedaah— Rebekah's state at the well (living, about to marry) - Jdg 14:4awe'aviv we'immo lo yadu ki mYHWH hi — parents' state of ignorance at a specific moment - 1 Kgs 1:4 wehamelek lo yedaah— David's state at the moment of Abishag's introduction - Jdg 11:39bwehi lo-yadah ish — daughter's state at the vow-fulfillment moment

Analysis: Every WX-QATAL parallel (including the grammar-reference library's own catalog) supports the attendant-state reading. v1's reading of 11:39b as "she lived a lifetime of subsequent celibacy" is grammatically unsupported. The clause says: at the moment the vow was fulfilled, her condition was virginal. This fits the sacrifice reading more naturally than the dedication reading (which would more naturally use Hannah-register dedication vocabulary).

docs.title: Pattern 3: LXX evidence across all relevant verses uniformly does NOT support disjunctive reading
docs.text:
Supporting verses: - LXX Jdg 11:31 A-text: καί (conjunctive) - LXX Jdg 11:31 B-text: asyndeton (neither καί nor ἤ) - LXX Jdg 5:11: διηγέομαι (recount, celebrative) - LXX Jdg 11:40: θρηνεῖν (lament) - LXX Jdg 11:34: BOTH ἀγαπητή AND μονογενής (maximal Akedah-echo) - LXX Exo 21:16, 21:20, Lev 5:1 (controls): ἤ freely used where Hebrew has o or disjunctive context

Analysis: Ancient Jewish translators had ἤ available and used it liberally for true disjunction. Its absence from Jdg 11:31 is substantive negative evidence against the disjunctive-waw reading. The LXX A-text's καί is a direct vote for the conjunctive "and offer him up" reading. Combined with Josephus (Ant. 5.7.6), the earliest Jewish reception is univocally literal-sacrifice.

docs.title: Pattern 4: The vow's vocabulary cluster is dedicated sacrificial-register
docs.text:
Supporting verses (Hiphil alah + olah collocation): - Gen 8:20 (Noah): animal sacrifice - Gen 22:2, 13 (Akedah): attempted human / actual ram - Lev 17:8 (law): legitimate sacrifice - 2 Kgs 3:27 (Mesha): actual human sacrifice (Moabite) - 2 Chr 1:6 (Solomon): 1000 animal sacrifices

Analysis: Every canonical occurrence of Hiphil alah + olah refers to actual burnt-offering presentation — animal or (in the two human cases) human. None refers to dedication, consecration, or lifelong service. This is lexical coherence across the corpus. Jephthah's vow is inside this cluster, not outside it.

docs.title: Pattern 5: Spirit-empowerment + subsequent moral failure is a Judges-era norm
docs.text:
Supporting verses: - Jdg 6:34 (Gideon Spirit) + 8:27 (gold ephod idolatry) - Jdg 13:25; 14:6, 19; 15:14 (Samson Spirit) + 14:1-3 (Philistine wife); 16:1 (harlot) - Jdg 11:29 (Jephthah Spirit) + 11:30-31 (rash vow) + 11:39 (its execution) - 1 Sam 10:10 (Saul Spirit) + 1 Sam 15 (disobedience) + 1 Sam 19:23-24 (disintegration)

Analysis: Clarke's "Spirit-of-YHWH could not permit Jephthah to imbrue his hands" argument runs aground on the book's own pattern. Spirit empowers deliverance; it does not morally ratify subsequent conduct.

docs.title: Pattern 6: Human sacrifice is a categorical Torah abomination
docs.text:
Supporting verses: Lev 18:21; 20:2-5; Deut 12:29-31; 18:9-12; Jer 7:31; 19:5; 32:35; 2 Kgs 16:3; 21:6; 23:10; Ezek 16:20-21; 20:26, 31; 23:37-39; Ps 106:37-38; Isa 57:5; Mic 6:7.

Analysis: The biblical witness against human sacrifice is univocal and categorical. No private-vow exception is permitted. Jer 7:31's weloalta al-libbi foreclose any reading in which YHWH accepted the act. But this prohibition is moral, not narrative-factual: the prohibition establishes that if Jephthah sacrificed his daughter, he did it in defiance of Torah — not that he could not have done it.

docs.title: 3. Word Study Integration: Register Table
docs.text:
Register Keyword cluster OT exemplars Jephthah used? A. DEDICATION natan laYHWH + kol yemei chayyav 1 Sam 1:11 (Hannah/Samuel); Num 3:9; 8:16 (Levites) NO B. REDEMPTION/VALUATION yaphli neder + beerkekha` (H6187) Lev 27:1-8 (persons vowed) NO C. FIRSTBORN CONSECRATION qaddesh (H6942 Piel) + peter rechem Exo 13:2 (firstborn-consecrate) NO D. NAZARITE nazar (H5144) + separation vocabulary Num 6; 1 Sam 1:11 (Samuel's hair); Jdg 13 (Samson); Lk 1:15 (JBap) NO E. JUDICIAL BAN charam + cherem (H2763/H2764) Num 21:2 Hormah; Josh 6-7 Jericho/Achan; 1 Sam 15 Amalek NO F. SACRIFICE Hiphil alah (H5927) + olah (H5930) Gen 8:20 Noah; Gen 22:2, 13 Akedah; 2 Kgs 3:27 Mesha; 2 Chr 1:6 Solomon YES — Jdg 11:31

Verdict: Five Torah-authenticated vocabularies for lawful lifelong-or-redeemable person-dedication existed. Jephthah used none. He reached instead for the dedicated sacrificial collocation F — and F has no non-sacrificial instance in the canonical corpus. The lexical verdict is: the vow-utterance is sacrificial.

Zabach absence at v.39. v1's strongest argument was the narrator's refusal to use zabach (H2076) at v.39. This is real, but it proves less than v1 claims. zabach is the slaughter-verb specifically tied to the zevach (communal meal-sacrifice) category; it is NOT the required verb for reporting olah-class burnt offerings. In 2 Kgs 3:27 (the closest parallel — Mesha's son), the narrator uses Hiphil alah + olah, NOT zabach. So the absence of zabach at Jdg 11:39 is expected for a olah-class offering; it is not a diagnostic signal against sacrifice. What's notable is the absence of even Hiphil alah + olah at v.39 — but as argued above, the narrator's compression wayyaas... et-nidro*refers to* the vow-text where Hiphilalah+olah` is explicit. The reader is expected to read v.39 through v.31's vow.

docs.title: Hebrews 11:32 inclusion
docs.text:
Greek parsing of Heb 11:32-34 aorists (see 02-verses.md section G, and grammar-reference/greek/mood-aorist-imperative.md): nine aorist verbs, all military-deliverance semantic field, each mapping to a specific deliverance-act by the listed figures. None maps to vow-execution. Jephthah's commended faith-act is the Ammonite victory (Jdg 11:32-33), not the vow.

Supporting logic of Heb 11 commending discrete acts: Samson's inclusion despite his moral failures; Gideon's despite the ephod; David's despite Bathsheba; Rahab's despite her prior trade. The faith roll commends specific faith-moments, not integrated characters.

Verdict: Heb 11:32 is SYMMETRICAL on the vow question. The NT neither endorses nor repudiates the vow's execution; it commends Jephthah's faith-driven deliverance of Israel from Ammon. The vow's ethics must be evaluated on OT grounds alone.

docs.title: Genesis 22 Akedah vs Jdg 11
docs.text:
The Akedah supplies the exact lexical template (yechidkha + Hiphil alah + olah) that Jdg 11 replicates — but the Akedah is the arrested archetype. Jdg 11 is the arrest-less, tragic inverse: the judge-warrior has no divine command to make the vow, no divine voice from heaven to halt it, no ram in the thicket. The absence of the arresting voice is itself the point: YHWH did not command the sacrifice (per Jer 7:31), does not accept it, and does not intervene to stop it. Judges-era Israel is on its own; and this is what "on its own" produces.

Note: Under the dedication reading, the Akedah parallel is cited in favor — "the arrest came in the form of a tacit Lev 27 redemption." But this requires the narrator to omit the arrest in a context where he elsewhere supplies it (Samson's several deliverances, Gideon's sign-seeking). The narrator's silence is damning for dedication and probative for sacrifice.

docs.title: 1 Samuel 14 vs Judges 11
docs.text: Saul's rash oath nearly killed Jonathan, but community intervention saved him. The absence of any such intervention in Jdg 11 fits the Judges-era pattern of moral collapse (Jdg 17:6; 21:25 refrain). The community did not intervene because communal moral sense had disintegrated — not because there was nothing to intervene about.

docs.title: NT reception generally
docs.text: The NT never cites Jdg 11:39 as a model of obedience or piety. Paul's references to "obeying God" rather than parents (Acts 5:29) use Peter and John, not Jephthah. Hebrews commends Jephthah for faith in battle, not vow-keeping. Jesus' teaching on oaths (Mt 5:33-37) and James' teaching (Jas 5:12) both cut against the oath-absolutism that drove Jephthah's tragedy: "Swear not at all... let your yea be yea and your nay, nay; for whatsoever is more than these cometh of evil." James 5:12 is a direct, if indirect, critique of vow-driven conduct like Jephthah's.

docs.title: 5. Difficult Passages / Counter-Arguments (the dedication reading's strongest forms)
Counter-argument A1: Disjunctive-waw in the vow
docs.text:
Strongest form: Clarke/Henry argue that Hebrew waw is polyfunctional; that Exo 21:15 ("smiteth his father or his mother") shows disjunctive waw; that therefore wehayah laYHWH wehaalitihu olah can mean "shall be to YHWH OR I will offer it up" with animal-or-human determining which clause applies.

Biblical rebuttal: 1. Exo 21:15 is a noun-level disjunction in an enumerative list, not an inter-clausal verbal disjunction in a vow apodosis. The grammatical categories are distinct. 2. Hebrew has a dedicated disjunctive particle o (H176). The same author of Judges uses it at Jdg 11:34 (ben o-bat) — three verses after the contested waw. He knew the particle; he didn't use it. 3. LXX A-text translates with καί (conjunctive); B-text with asyndeton. Neither uses ἤ (the Greek disjunctive). The earliest Jewish translators did not read disjunction. 4. In the canonical OT corpus, there is NO unambiguous case of a vow-apodosis chain carrying disjunctive force between two weqatal clauses. Jdg 11:31 would be a hapax.

Verdict: The disjunctive-waw reading is grammatically possible but marked, with no supporting parallel and against the LXX witness. Net: weak evidence for dedication.

docs.title: Counter-argument A2: hayyotze could be an animal
docs.text:
Strongest form: The masc. sg. participle hayyotze is maximally indefinite; a Gileadite house-courtyard could accommodate livestock; Josephus (Ant. 5.7.10) paraphrases as "what living creature soever" — implicitly acknowledging ambiguity.

Biblical rebuttal: 1. The H3318 yatsa word study (04-word-studies.md Part 2) reports ZERO animal referents for masc. sg. hayyotze in the OT; the construction skews strongly human. 2. The idiom liqra'ti ("to meet me") is a social-encounter construction used across ~100 OT occurrences, essentially always for persons (Gen 14:17; Gen 24:17, 65; Num 22:34; Josh 9:11; Jdg 4:22; 1 Sam 6:13; etc.). Animals in the OT do not "come out to meet" returning warriors. 3. Even Josephus (who held the literal-sacrifice view) does not read the grammar as disjunctive — his "living creature" is a paraphrastic widening of the referent class, not a grammatical disjunction of the predicate.

Verdict: The vow's grammar already presupposed a human greeter. Jephthah's shock (v.35) is not at "human vs animal" — it is at which human greeted him (his only child). Net: weak evidence for dedication.

docs.title: Counter-argument A3: Lev 27:1-8 redemption via Phinehas (Targum Jonathan)
docs.text:
Strongest form: Jephthah's vow was a neder, which under Lev 27:1-8 is always redeemable by priestly valuation; Phinehas was alive and accessible (Jdg 20:27-28); the Targum specifically indicts Jephthah for not consulting Phinehas. So perhaps Phinehas redeemed her tacitly.

Biblical rebuttal: 1. The Targum's own framing is that Jephthah DID sacrifice her AND was wrong NOT to have consulted Phinehas for redemption. The Targum is a literal-sacrifice reading with a halakhic commentary attached. 2. If Phinehas had redeemed her, the narrator would say so — exactly as 1 Sam 1:21-28 specifies Samuel's transfer to Shiloh and Eli's care. 3. Lev 27:1-8 redemption discharges the vow by silver paid to the sanctuary; the person then goes home. Jephthah's daughter did not go home — she returned to her father (11:39) who fulfilled his vow. "Fulfillment of a Lev 27 redemption" would be "he paid 30 shekels and she lived on." The text does not say that.

Verdict: The Phinehas-redemption scenario is consistent with ancient halakhic commentary on what Jephthah should have done, but not with what the narrative says he did. Net: weak evidence for dedication; strong evidence of a missed Torah off-ramp.

docs.title: Counter-argument A4: Figurative olah (total dedication)
docs.text:
Strongest form: Perhaps olah in Jdg 11:31 is figurative — total consecration, whole self given to YHWH as a "living burnt offering" (cf. Rom 12:1 thysia zosa).

Biblical rebuttal: 1. In the entire OT, olah (H5930) is never figurative. It is the technical term for the whole-burnt-offering (Lev 1). The NT metaphor (Rom 12:1) uses thysia (G2378) with zosa (living) — an explicit modifier that fundamentally changes the register. Jdg 11:31 has no such modifier. 2. The Hiphil alah + olah collocation in the OT corpus has no non-cultic, non-literal occurrence.

Verdict: The figurative-olah reading requires imposing NT metaphor onto OT cultic vocabulary. It is an exegetical projection, not a reading supported by OT usage. Net: weak evidence for dedication.

docs.title: Counter-argument A5: Perpetual-virginity institution via Exo 38:8 / 1 Sam 2:22 / Anna
docs.text:
Strongest form: Women served at the tabernacle door; Anna in Lk 2:36-37 lived in the temple; perhaps Jephthah's daughter joined such an institution.

Biblical rebuttal: 1. Henry (9412) is right: no OT law or custom establishes celibacy-as-religion. The women at the tabernacle door (Exo 38:8; 1 Sam 2:22) are not described as vowed virgins; they are hatsov'ot (assembling women), possibly married. 2. Anna's NT example postdates Jephthah by over a millennium; she was widowed, not a lifelong virgin by vow. 3. If a vowed-virginity institution existed in Jephthah's day, the narrator would mention her being sent to it. The narrative has her going to the mountains — not to a sanctuary — to mourn; then returning to her father, who fulfilled his vow. This is not the trajectory of a dedicated-to-sanctuary woman.

Verdict: No OT basis for a vowed-virginity institution in Jephthah's era. Net: weak evidence for dedication.

docs.title: Counter-argument A6: Father's annulment (Num 30)
docs.text:
Strongest form: Perhaps Jephthah annulled his own vow via some Num 30-adjacent principle.

Biblical rebuttal: 1. Num 30:2 binds a man's own vow unconditionally; father's annulment applies to daughter's vows, not father's. 2. Jephthah's own conviction at 11:35 (lo ukhal lashuv) excludes this: he does not believe he can annul.

Verdict: Weak evidence; internal contradiction with Jephthah's own testimony.

docs.title: Counter-argument A7: Spirit-presence entails divine approval
docs.text:
Strongest form: Clarke argues the Spirit of YHWH in 11:29 would not permit a sacrificial act.

Biblical rebuttal: See Pattern 5 above. Samson's Spirit + Philistine wife; Gideon's Spirit + ephod-idolatry; Saul's Spirit + disintegration. The Judges-era book pattern is Spirit-for-deliverance, not Spirit-for-moral-ratification.

Verdict: Theologically appealing but textually unsupportable. Net: neutralized.

docs.title: Counter-argument A8: NT praise (Heb 11:32)
docs.text:
Strongest form: If Jephthah sacrificed his daughter, how can Hebrews commend him?

Biblical rebuttal: See Section 4 above. Heb 11 commends discrete faith-acts, not whole biographies. Samson, Gideon, David all included despite grave moral failures; the faith-act for Jephthah is the Ammonite military deliverance, not the vow.

Verdict: The NT praise does not entail endorsement of the vow's execution. Net: neutralized.

docs.title: Counter-argument A9: Narrator's lexical restraint at v.39 (v1's strongest leg)
docs.text:
Strongest form: The narrator at 11:39 used bare asah, avoiding every sacrifice-verb; in every explicit human-sacrifice report the narrator names the act. Silence here is diagnostic.

Biblical rebuttal: 1. The narrator is explicitly cross-referencing v.31: et-nidro asher nadar. The compression does not signal non-sacrifice; it signals read the vow-text to know what "doing the vow" means. 2. The narrator's restraint is at the climactic moment where a skilled ancient narrator (Judges-author) naturally performs referential compression. This is a literary feature, not a theological signal. 3. The narrator's follow-up gloss in v.39b (wehi lo-yadah ish) is state-at-moment (WX-QATAL), not subsequent-lifetime. It comments on her virgin state AT THE FULFILLMENT, tying back to her two-month mourning overbethulim` (vv.37-38). This fits sacrifice (she died a virgin) far more naturally than dedication (she lived on as a virgin — for which the narrator would have used the Samuel-template vocabulary).

Verdict: The lexical-restraint argument is real but overstated. Net: weak-to-moderate evidence for dedication; counter-explained by the referential-compression hypothesis.

docs.title: Counter-argument A10: letannot as celebrative recount
docs.text:
Strongest form: The dis legomenon tanah in Jdg 5:11 is celebrative ("recount the righteous acts of YHWH"); the same verb in 11:40 should follow the Jdg 5:11 control.

Biblical rebuttal: 1. A dis legomenon with one celebrative occurrence does not lock the other occurrence into the same register. Semantic range of rare verbs is precisely what they don't establish. 2. LXX Jdg 11:40 chose θρηνεῖν (lament), committing to a funerary reading. The earliest Jewish translators understood 11:40 as lament. 3. An annual four-day rite of women-only mourning fits Near Eastern young-woman-memorial patterns (cf. Tammuz in Ezek 8:14). A four-day commemorative recounting of a lifelong dedication has no parallel in the OT.

Verdict: The letannot argument is linguistically possible but not probable; the LXX choice of θρηνεῖν, plus rite-structure, weights lament. Net: weak evidence for dedication.

docs.title: 6. Preliminary Synthesis
docs.text:
Weighing the evidence:

For the literal-sacrifice reading (B' in the prompt framework: "sinful rash vow that should NOT have been fulfilled, executed anyway"):

  1. The vow-utterance (Jdg 11:31) uses the dedicated Hiphil alah + olah sacrificial collocation. Every other canonical OT occurrence of this collocation refers to actual burnt offering (animal or, in Mesha's case, human).
  2. The vow-utterance does NOT use any of the five Torah-authenticated dedication/redemption/consecration/nazirite/ban vocabularies.
  3. LXX witness is uniform: καί or asyndeton at v.31, never ἤ. Earliest Jewish translators read conjunctively.
  4. Josephus (Ant. 5.7.6) — first-century Jewish reception — reads literal sacrifice and declares it Torah-violating.
  5. Jephthah's own response at 11:35 (lo ukhal lashuv + tearing of clothes) indicates he read the vow as irrevocable and destructive.
  6. The narrator's bare asah at v.39 is referential compression pointing back to v.31's sacrificial vocabulary, not lexical denial of sacrifice.
  7. The WX-QATAL wehi lo-yadah ish` at v.39b is attendant-state (grammar-library verdict), comporting with "she died a virgin at the vow's fulfillment."
  8. The narrator's silence on a post-fulfillment life (contrast Samuel's post-dedication narrative in 1 Sam 2:11, 18, 26) is evidence that no such life occurred.
  9. The annual four-day letannot rite is most naturally lament (LXX θρηνεῖν) for a young woman who died before marriage.
  10. No Torah law, custom, or usage establishes a vowed-virginity institution in Jephthah's era.
  11. Jephthah's failure to invoke Lev 5:4-5 (guilt-offering) or Lev 27:1-8 (redemption via Phinehas) is narratively coherent with his own lo ukhal lashuv absolutism.
  12. The Judges-era pattern (Spirit + subsequent moral failure — Samson, Gideon) makes Spirit-presence non-probative of the vow's righteousness.
  13. Human sacrifice is a categorical Torah abomination (Lev 18:21; 20:2-5; Deut 12:31; 18:10; Jer 7:31). Jephthah's act violates Torah regardless of his intent. Jer 7:31's weloalta al-libbi precludes divine acceptance but not human execution.
  14. Heb 11:32 is symmetrical — commending Jephthah's military faith, not his vow-execution.

For the dedication reading:

  1. Narrator's lexical restraint at v.39 (but see #6 above for the literary-compression counter).
  2. wehi lo-yadah ish` at v.39b (but see #7 above for the grammar-library counter).
  3. bethulim mourning (but this fits both readings: she mourns her state-at-death or her state-going-forward; the state-at-death reading is actually more linguistically natural given the two-month window before execution).
  4. letannot as celebrative (but see counter A10).
  5. No community override (ambiguous — fits Judges moral-collapse arc either way).
  6. Heb 11:32 inclusion (neutralized by faith-act selectivity).
  7. Torah's categorical human-sacrifice prohibition (this argues what Jephthah should have done, not what he did; Judges narrates what people did, often in violation of Torah).

Weight: The literal-sacrifice reading wins on the Hebrew lexicon (registers), the Hebrew syntax (WX-QATAL circumstantial does not carry durative force per the library's own entry), the LXX witness (no disjunctive reading), the internal narrative (no post-fulfillment life-story), and the book-level pattern (Judges-era moral collapse with Spirit-empowered-but-flawed judges). The dedication reading wins on: narrator's restraint at v.39 (counter-explained), letannot semantic preference (counter-explained by LXX), and the a priori theological discomfort with a literal reading.

The theological discomfort is the strongest force behind the dedication reading — but it is precisely the discomfort Judges is designed to induce. Judges tells a story of Israel's moral and religious disintegration under no-king conditions. Jephthah's tragedy is one data point in that story. The book does not soften Eglon's bowel voiding, Jael's tent-peg, or the Levite's dismembered concubine; there is no reason to expect it to soften Jephthah's sacrifice either. What Judges gives us is an unflinching look at what happens when Israel is without king and without law: the judges themselves become morally entangled with the disorder they are supposed to address. Jephthah's faith was real (Heb 11:32); his military victory was real (Jdg 11:32-33); and his sinful, Torah-violating, rash-vow-driven sacrifice of his only daughter was also real — and reported to us precisely so we see what Israel looked like in the dark era between Joshua and Samuel.

Analysis written: 2026-04-17 Based on: PROMPT.md, 00-references.md, 01-topics.md, 02-verses.md, 04-word-studies.md, grammar-reference library entries (syntax-wx-qatal-circumstantial.md, lxx-disjunctive-waw-vows.md, syntax-conditional-vow.md, weqatal-apodosis-vow.md, syntax-waw-conjunction.md, passages/jdg-11-30-31.md, passages/jdg-11-37-40.md), 20 word-study library entries.

docs.title: Word Studies: Jephthah's Vow Lexicon
docs.text: All entries in this study have canonical library files; fresh search_strongs.py lookups were NOT needed. This file SUMMARIZES each library entry and highlights the data most load-bearing for the question. File paths are cited for the analysis agent to follow up.

docs.title: Part 1: Lexical Choices in Jdg 11:31 — Register Comparison Table
docs.text:
The most decisive word-level datum in this study is the lexical register Jephthah's vow belongs to, compared to known OT dedication-vows and to known OT sacrificial commands. Four vocabulary clusters define four registers; Jdg 11:31 tracks cluster 3 (sacrificial), not cluster 1 (dedication) or cluster 2 (redemption/valuation).

Cluster Verb + object collocation OT exemplars Lifelong-service? Sacrifice? 1. DEDICATION/LIVING-SERVICE נתן (natan) la-YHWH + כָּל־יְמֵי חַיָּיו 1 Sam 1:11 (Hannah/Samuel); Num 3:9, 8:16, 8:19 (Levites "netunim laYHWH") YES (living service at sanctuary) NO 2. REDEMPTION/VALUATION יַפְלִא נֶדֶר + בְּעֶרְכְּךָ (Lev 27:2) Lev 27:1-8 (priestly age/sex-graded tariff — vowed persons redeemed by silver) (person goes home, silver paid) NO — explicitly redeemable 3. SACRIFICE/IMMOLATION עלה Hiphil (ha'alah) + עולה (olah) Gen 8:20 (Noah); Gen 22:2, 13 (Akedah command + ram); Lev 17:8; 2 Kgs 3:27 (Mesha's son — wayya'alehu olah); 2 Chr 1:6 NO YES — technical cultic collocation for burnt-offering presentation 4. FIRSTBORN CONSECRATION קדש Piel (qaddesh) + כָּל־פֶּטֶר רֶחֶם (firstborn) Exo 13:2 (sanctify every firstborn) — firstborn son REDEEMED by lamb/5 shekels (Exo 13:13, Num 18:15-16) — REDEEMED, not sacrificed

docs.title: Jephthah's vow uses Cluster 3:
docs.text:
Jdg 11:31 verbatim: וְהָיָה לַיהוָה וְהַעֲלִיתִהוּ עֹלָה

  • וְהָיָה לַיהוָה ("shall be to YHWH") — plain hayah + dative; NOT Hannah's natan + kol yemei chayyav; NOT Lev 27:2's yaphli neder + be'erkekha; NOT Exo 13:2's qaddesh + kol peter rechem
  • וְהַעֲלִיתִהוּ עֹלָה (Hiphil perfect weqatal עלה + noun עולה) — this is the technical cultic burnt-offering collocation. Every other canonical OT occurrence of this exact verb-stem + object-noun pairing (Noah, Abraham's ram in the thicket, Mesha the Moabite's son) reports an ACTUAL burnt-offering presentation.

The lexical verdict on the vow-utterance: Jephthah chose the register of sacrifice, not dedication. If he had meant "lifelong dedication," the Hannah-register lexicon was available and familiar; he did not deploy it.

However: The VOW UTTERANCE (v. 31) and the VOW EXECUTION (v. 39) employ different lexicons: - Jephthah's OWN UTTERANCE (v. 31): SACRIFICIAL-register (Hiphil alah + olah) - The NARRATOR'S REPORT OF EXECUTION (v. 39): NEUTRAL-register (wayya'as lah = "he did to her")

The narrator's deliberate lexical restraint at v. 39 is the disjunctive hinge of the whole debate — see Part 3 below on H2076 zabach.

docs.title: Part 2: Word Studies From the Library
H5087 — נָדַר (nadar) "to vow"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H5087-nadar.md - 28 unique verses, 35 tokens — always Qal - Always paired with cognate noun H5088 neder (figura etymologica "vow a vow" = נָדַר נֶדֶר) - First occurrence: Gen 28:20 (Jacob); key instances: Num 21:2 (Israel/Hormah); Jdg 11:30 (Jephthah); 1 Sam 1:11 (Hannah) - LXX bridge: G2172 εὔχομαι (primary) - Jephthah's vow at Jdg 11:30 is formally a נֶדֶר — not a חֵרֶם (cherem/ban) nor a שְׁבוּעָה (shebuah/oath). This formal classification matters for Lev 27 law: neder falls under Lev 27:1-8 (always redeemable via priestly 'erek); only cherem falls under Lev 27:28-29 (non-redeemable, death penalty).

docs.title: H5088 — נֶדֶר (neder) "a vow"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H5088-neder.md - 64 tokens in 57 unique verses; 15 books - Domain: conditional "if-then" promise, always directed to God; never to another human - Legal class distinct from נְדָבָה (nedabah, freewill offering) and from שְׁבוּעָה (shebuah, sworn oath) - Num 30 governs annulment of women's vows (father/husband authority); Num 30:2 governs A MAN'S own vow — "he shall not break his word" - LXX bridge: G2171 εὐχή - Jephthah's vow (Jdg 11:30) is explicitly classified as neder by the narrator ("vayyiddar Yiphtach NEDER laYHWH"). Distinguishing from cherem is decisive for the Lev 27 framework.

docs.title: H5927 — עָלָה (ʻalah) "go up / [Hiphil] offer up"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H5927-alah.md - 817 unique verses (1,122 tokens) — one of the most frequent OT verbs - Stem distribution: Qal 606 (intransitive motion), Hiphil 262 (causative — "bring up / offer up" cultic) - Semantic category #4 "Cultic offering": Gen 8:20 (Noah post-flood); Gen 22:2 (Abraham Akedah); Gen 22:13 (ram); Lev 2:12; 2 Chr 1:6 (Solomon's 1000 burnt offerings) - Jdg 11:31 וְהַעֲלִיתִהוּ (Hiphil.Perf.1s+3ms) "I will cause it/him to go up" — a Hiphil ascent clause with a personal-suffix object in a sacrificial context. The Hiphil + pronominal-suffix + עולה collocation appears in parallels that DO report literal burnt-offerings (2 Kgs 3:27 Mesha). - LXX bridge: G305 ἀναβαίνω / G399 ἀναφέρω (the latter specifically for Hiphil "offer up," → Heb 7:27, 1 Pet 2:24).

docs.title: H5930 — עֹלָה (ʻolah) "burnt offering"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H5930-olah.md - 302 tokens in 262 unique verses; 23 books - "The sacrifice that goes up entirely to God... no portion retained by priest or worshipper; the whole animal is consumed on the altar." - Torah-regulated: Lev 1:3-17 (founding Torah of the burnt-offering) — "male without blemish"; "hand-laying"; "flaying, washing of inwards and legs"; "all burned" - 96% of KJV renderings = "burnt offering"; the verb + noun pair עלה Hiphil + olah is the irreducibly cultic collocation - LXX bridge: G3646 ὁλοκαύτωμα (from "holos" + "kaio" — ALL-BURNED, whence English "holocaust"). Heb 10:6, 8 quotes Ps 40:6 LXX; Heb 7:27, 1 Pet 2:24 use ἀναφέρω for the Hiphil alah + olah action. - Jdg 11:31 uses olah as the adverbial accusative object of Hiphil alah: עֹלָה — "as a burnt offering." This SPECIFIES the mode of offering; it is not "dedication" but "total-consumption burnt-offering."

docs.title: H2076 — זָבַח (zabach) "slaughter / sacrifice"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H2076-zabach.md - 127 unique verses (149 tokens); 24 books - Standard OT verb for the slaughter of sacrifice — the KILL-STEP in cultic offering - CRITICALLY ABSENT from Jdg 11:39. The narrator does NOT say "וַיִּזְבַּח אֶת־בִּתּוֹ" ("and he sacrificed his daughter"). The narrator's lexical restraint at v. 39 avoids zabach, shachat (technical priestly slaughter), saraph (burn), and even the Hiphil alah + olah collocation Jephthah himself used at v. 31. - LXX bridge: G2380 θύω (primary). - See also word-studies/H5930-olah.md row "Easily confused words" — zabach (slaughter, with slaughter-event emphasis) vs. olah (the whole-burn noun-class). Heb 10:5-10 uses both concepts.

docs.title: H2763 — חָרַם (charam) "devote / utterly destroy"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H2763-charam.md - 57 tokens in 48 verses; "devote to religious uses (especially destruction); consecrate" - PRIMARY Hiphil usage = judicial ban in conquest (Josh 6:17, Jericho; Num 21:2-3, Hormah; 1 Sam 15:3, Amalek) - Lev 27:29 + Lev 27:28 use Hophal ("shall be devoted") to mark the non-redeemable category - Num 21:2's apodosis is וְהַחֲרַמְתִּי אֶת־עָרֵיהֶם — the vow uses charam, not olah. Therefore Num 21 is a different vow-class from Jdg 11; Num 21 is lawful conquest-ban on an enemy's cities.

docs.title: H2764 — חֵרֶם (cherem) "ban / devoted thing"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H2764-cherem.md - 39 tokens in 31 verses; the NOUN form of the ban-class - Lev 27:28-29 is the key legal text: cherem is NOT redeemable; cherem-devoted persons are PUT TO DEATH. - The cherem category covers: apostate cities (Deut 13:15), Molech-worshipers (Lev 20:2-5), Amalek/conquest-enemies, Achan (Josh 7). - CRITICAL LEGAL DISTINCTION: Lev 27:1-8 (ordinary neder — redeemable by priest-valuation) ≠ Lev 27:28-29 (cherem — not redeemable, death-penalty). Jephthah's vow at Jdg 11:30 is explicitly a neder (H5088), NOT a cherem. Therefore Lev 27:28-29 does NOT authorize Jephthah's daughter's death; Lev 27:1-8 applies and authorizes priestly redemption.

docs.title: H1330 — בְּתוּלָה (bethulah) "virgin"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H1330-bthuwlah.md - 50 unique verses, 51 tokens - Primary meaning: sexually inexperienced young woman - Figurative: personified city or nation (virgin daughter of Zion, etc.) - Gen 24:16 is the controlling definition: "bethulah, neither had any man known her" — explicit gloss showing bethulah alone doesn't always specify sexual inexperience without clarifying context; the standard clarification is the lo-yada-ish formula - LXX bridge: G3933 παρθένος - Note from library: "Highest concentration in lament/exile literature (Lam, Jer, Amos, Joel) and Persian court narrative (Esther)." Lam 1:4 and 2:10 have virgins mourning in the temple — a LIVING-virgin-mourning idiom parallel to Jdg 11:37-38.

docs.title: H1331 — בְּתוּלִים (bethulim) "virginity [state or tokens]"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H1331-bthuwliym.md - 10 tokens in 9 verses — ALL in Lev 21:13; Deut 22:14-20; Ezek 23:3, 8; Jdg 11:37-38 - Plural of abstraction / condition: "state of virginity, tokens of virginity" - The plural ending -îm here = abstract state (like נְעוּרִים "youth," זְקֻנִים "old age") - EVERY canonical occurrence describes a LIVING woman's virginity-state: - Lev 21:13 — priestly marriage qualification (living bride) - Deut 22:14-20 — bride-defamation trial tokens (living woman on trial) - Ezek 23:3, 8 — "breasts of their virginity" (figurative youth of Israel, but living-subject imagery) - Jdg 11:37-38 — Jephthah's daughter mourning her bethulim for two months on the mountains - NO OT occurrence describes a deceased woman's past virginity. Mourning OVER bethulim (as Jephthah's daughter does) is NOT a mourning of impending death; it is mourning the CONTINUING STATE OF UNMARRIED CHILDLESSNESS. - LXX bridge: G3932 παρθενία (used in Luke 2:36 of Anna — "from her virginity" meaning her pre-married years).

docs.title: H3045 — יָדַע (yada') "know [incl. sexually]"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H3045-yada.md - 873 unique verses (1,099 tokens); one of the most frequent OT verbs - Category 4 (sexual euphemism): ~15 verses — Gen 4:1, 17, 25; Gen 19:5, 8; Gen 24:16; Gen 38:26; Num 31:17-18, 35; Jdg 11:39; Jdg 19:25; 21:11-12; 1 Kgs 1:4 - The FEMININE formulation (feminine subject + lo yada' ish) is rare — Gen 24:16 Rebekah; Num 31:17-18, 35 Midianite women; Jdg 11:39 Jephthah's daughter; Jdg 21:12 Jabesh-Gilead virgins; 1 Kgs 1:4 Abishag (inverted — masculine subject "the king knew her not") - Every other occurrence of the feminine formulation describes a LIVING woman.

docs.title: H5144 — נָזַר (nazar) "separate / consecrate"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H5144-nazar.md - Root of Nazarite (H5139 nazir). Num 6:1-21 establishes the Nazarite law: separation-by-vow to YHWH. - Samson (Jdg 13:5, 7; 16:17), Samuel (via Hannah's vow 1 Sam 1:11 — "no razor upon his head"), Rechabites (Jer 35), John the Baptist (Lk 1:15) are instances. - All Nazarites serve YHWH WHILE LIVING — separation is for life-service, not immolation. - Nazarite category provides a ready biblical framework for "living consecration" if Jephthah's daughter were dedicated as such (with mountain-pilgrimage mourning her bethulim = unmarried state accompanying dedication).

docs.title: H8567 — תָּנָה (tanah) "recount / commemorate / [KJV] lament"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H8567-tanah.md - DIS LEGOMENON — only 2 Piel occurrences: Jdg 5:11 (יְתַנּוּ) and Jdg 11:40 (לְתַנּוֹת) - Jdg 5:11 control context: שָׁם יְתַנּוּ צִדְקוֹת יְהוָה — "there they rehearse/recount the righteous acts of the LORD" — CELEBRATIVE recital at the watering places, after YHWH's victory through Deborah - Jdg 11:40 parallel construction: annual four-day rite לְתַנּוֹת לְבַת־יִפְתָּח — "to recount/commemorate for the daughter of Jephthah" - LXX splits the two: Jdg 5:11 → διηγέομαι (G1334 "recount, narrate"); Jdg 11:40 → θρηνεῖν (G2354 "lament, sing a dirge"). The Greek translators chose opposing emotional registers for the same Hebrew verb — reflecting their interpretive COMMITMENT to the literal-sacrifice reading at Jdg 11:40. - Syntactic frame: ל (of interest/benefit) + בַת־יִפְתָּח, NOT עַל (of object-of-mourning). One mourns עַל someone dead (Gen 37:35; 2 Sam 1:24); one recounts ל (for/to) someone being commemorated. This frame-preposition (ל, not עַל) supports "recount/commemorate" over "lament." - Jdg 5:11 control governs the dis legomenon → commemorative-celebration reading is lexically on-point; KJV "lament" is interpretive importation from the literal-sacrifice reading of the surrounding narrative, not from the verb itself.

docs.title: H2403 — חַטָּאָה (chattaah) "sin / sin-offering"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H2403-chattaah.md - Relevant for Lev 5:4-13 — the rash-oath guilt-offering atonement pathway. Lev 5:6 specifies a female from the flock as a sin offering for a rash oath unwittingly sworn. This off-ramp was available to Jephthah.

docs.title: H3173 — יָחִיד (yachid) "only [child / darling / solitary]"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H3173-yachiyd.md - 12 OT occurrences; 7 books - Clustered in sacrifice-of-only-child contexts (5 of 12 = 42%): - Gen 22:2, 12, 16 — Isaac at the Akedah (averted) - Jdg 11:34 — Jephthah's daughter (contested) - Zech 12:10 — the pierced Messianic one (prophetic) - Jdg 11:34 is the ONLY OT verse where LXX renders yachid with BOTH ἀγαπητή (G27 "beloved") AND μονογενής (G3439 "only-begotten") — importing the full Akedah-echo vocabulary into the Jephthah narrative. - The NT Christological titles μονογενής (John 1:14, 18; 3:16; 1 John 4:9; Heb 11:17 of Isaac) and ἀγαπητός (Mat 3:17; 17:5 — of Jesus at baptism/transfiguration) both trace back to this yachid word-family. - Lexical echo Jdg 11 ↔ Gen 22: yachid (Jdg 11:34) + Hiphil alah + olah (Jdg 11:31) = yechidkha (Gen 22:2) + Hiphil alah + olah (Gen 22:2). This is the TIGHTEST intertextual link in Jdg 11 — the narrator is cuing the Akedah.

docs.title: H6187 — עֵרֶךְ (ʻerek) "valuation / estimation"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H6187-erek.md - 35 tokens in 29 verses; 20 in Leviticus (Lev 27 redemption tariff + Lev 5 trespass) - Lev 27:1-8 establishes the canonical Torah off-ramp: when a person is vowed to YHWH, the priest assigns a silver valuation calibrated by age and sex: - Adult male 20-60 = 50 shekels - Adult female 20-60 = 30 shekels - Female 5-20 = 10 shekels - Poverty clause (Lev 27:8) scales the tariff to the vower's ability to pay. NO ONE is excluded from the redemption off-ramp. - LXX bridge: G5092 τιμή ("price, value"). NT echo 1 Cor 6:20 "ye are bought with a price (τιμή)"; 1 Pet 1:18-19. - Library entry explicitly states: "The category [Lev 27:1-8 'erek] EXPLICITLY CONTEMPLATES THE SCENARIO OF JDG 11 — a hastily-sworn person-vow. Torah's answer is: pay the priestly valuation. The silver discharges the vow; the person goes home." - This is the legal ground for the Targum Jonathan / Phinehas argument (via Clarke 28231): Jephthah's fault lay in not invoking Lev 27:1-8 redemption via priestly consultation with Phinehas (Jdg 20:27-28 places Phinehas at the Ark in the immediately-preceding Benjamin war — chronologically accessible to Jephthah).

docs.title: H6942 — קָדַשׁ (qadash) "consecrate / sanctify"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H6942-qadash.md - 153 unique verses (186 tokens); 23 books - The DEDICATION-CONSECRATION verb. Used for: - Sacred TIME (Sabbath — Gen 2:3; Deut 5:12) - Sacred SPACE/OBJECTS (tabernacle Num 7:1; Solomon's court 1 Kgs 8:64) - Sacred PERSONS (firstborn Exo 13:2; prophets Jer 1:5; Levite priests Exo 19:10; Nazarites) - RITUAL SELF-PREPARATION (Josh 3:5; 1 Sam 16:5) - LXX bridge: G37 ἁγιάζω (119 co-occurrences — near-exclusive pairing). - Exo 13:2's firstborn-consecration uses Piel qaddesh — the lexical register of consecration proper: קַדֶּשׁ־לִי כָל־בְּכוֹר כָּל־פֶּטֶר רֶחֶם. Jdg 11:31 does NOT use qadash. - This confirms: Jephthah had THREE Torah-authenticated vocabularies for consecrating a person to YHWH for life-service available: (1) natan (Hannah); (2) 'erek valuation (Lev 27:1-8); (3) qaddesh (Exo 13:2 firstborn formula). He used none of them. His chosen vocabulary was Hiphil alah + olah = SACRIFICIAL register.

docs.title: H5414 — נָתַן (natan) "give / assign / dedicate"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H5414-nathan.md - 1,814 unique verses (2,285 tokens) — one of the top 10 most frequent OT verbs - Category 4 (dedication/vow): "giving persons or things over to YHWH": - 1 Sam 1:11 — "then I will give him (unetattiw) unto the LORD all the days of his life" (Hannah's vow formula — the paradigmatic LIVING-dedication construction) - Num 3:9, 8:16, 8:19 — the Levites are wholly "given" (netunim netunim) to YHWH in substitution for the firstborn — note the DOUBLED natan marks irrevocable dedication - Num 18:6 — Levites as "a gift (matanah) for the LORD, to do the service of the tabernacle" - Library entry explicitly states: "When a person or animal is natan-ed to YHWH, the verb marks transfer of custody — typically for ONGOING SERVICE (Levites, Samuel). When a sacrifice is made, Hebrew reaches for he'alah (H5927 hiphil, 'cause to ascend, offer up as burnt offering') or hiqrib (H7126 hiphil, 'bring near, present'). Jdg 11:31 (Jephthah's vow) uses wehayah laYHWH ('shall be to YHWH') + wehaʻalitihu ʻolah ('and I will offer it up a burnt offering') — it does NOT use natan. The switch from the Hannah/Levite natan-dedication pattern to the ʻolah-sacrificial pattern is the pivotal lexical datum whenever Jephthah's vow is analyzed."

docs.title: H3318 — יָצָא (yatsa') "go out / come forth"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H3318-yatsa.md - 1,069 tokens in 990 unique verses — 14th most frequent OT verb - Qal participle ms absolute (הַיּוֹצֵא) referent distribution (51 + 12 construct = 63 total occurrences): - Human individual (~14): named person exits a place - Human collective (~20): military/civic "going out" - Offspring from loins (4): Gen 15:4, 17:6, 1 Ki 8:19, 2 Chr 6:9 - YHWH/divine agent (5) - Inanimate/natural (15) - Animal subject: ZERO direct cases - Formulaic frame "whosoever cometh forth of the doors of X": Jdg 11:31 is the UNIQUE OT instance of הַיּוֹצֵא + "doors of my house" to-meet-me. Closest idiomatic neighbors (Num 27:17 "go out and come in"; 1 Ki 3:7; Prov 7:15 "came forth to meet") all describe HUMAN leadership/social movement. - Grammatical pattern יוֹצֵא + לִקְרָאתִי (going out to meet me) skews strongly toward human referent — "meeting" (qara / liqrat) is a social act performed by persons, not animals, across its ~100 OT occurrences. - CONTRA POINT: Josephus Ant. 5.7.10 paraphrases the vow-referent as "what LIVING CREATURE soever" — a generic/universal reading. The Gileadite house-courtyard setting could technically accommodate livestock. But the Hebrew GRAMMATICAL DEFAULT for this specific construction is HUMAN. - This datum pushes AGAINST the pure disjunctive-vow defense that says "Jephthah expected an animal and was surprised by a human" — lexically, the formulation already skewed human from the start.

docs.title: H7311 — רוּם (rum) "be high / exalt"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H7311-ruwm.md - Peripheral to this study; semantically close to H5927 עלה but STATIVE (be high) vs. DYNAMIC (move up). No direct impact on Jdg 11 analysis.

docs.title: H8199 — שָׁפַט (shaphat) "to judge"
docs.text: Source: D:/bible/bible-studies/word-studies/H8199-shaphat.md - Relevant for Jdg 11:27 ("the LORD the Judge be judge this day") and Jdg 12:7 ("Jephthah judged Israel six years"). Confirms Jephthah's canonical judge-status; post-vow narrative records no disqualification for him.

docs.title: Part 3: The Narrator's Lexical Restraint at Jdg 11:39 — The Diagnostic Absence
docs.text:
At the climax of the narrative, the narrator reports Jephthah's fulfillment with:

וַיַּעַשׂ לָהּ אֶת־נִדְרוֹ אֲשֶׁר נָדָר "and he did to her his vow which he had vowed" (Jdg 11:39)

The verb is עשׂה (asah) — bare "do/make" — chosen over ALL available sacrificial-reporting verbs that the same Hebrew author could have used:

Available Hebrew verb KJV gloss Used where reporting human immolation Used at Jdg 11:39? זבח (zabach, H2076) slaughter/sacrifice Lev 17:5; 1 Ki 14:23; Ps 106:37-38 (demons) ABSENT שׁחט (shachat, H7819) slaughter (technical) Gen 22:10 (Abraham about to kill Isaac); Lev priestly slaughter ABSENT שׂרף (saraph, H8313) burn Jer 7:31 "to burn their sons and daughters"; 2 Kgs 23:16 ABSENT עלה Hiphil + עולה offer up as burnt-offering 2 Kgs 3:27 Mesha: wayya'alehu olah ("he offered him up as burnt-offering") — EXACTLY the construction Jephthah used at 11:31 ABSENT הֶעֱבִיר בָּאֵשׁ (he'evir ba'esh) pass through fire 2 Kgs 16:3, 21:6; Jer 32:35; Deut 18:10 ABSENT נכה Hiphil (hikkah) smite/kill various ABSENT מות Hiphil (hemiit) put to death Lev 27:29 Hophal יוּמָת ("shall be put to death") ABSENT

The narrator had every standard Hebrew sacrifice-reporting verb available and chose NONE of them. Instead he used עשׂה (plain "do") and pointed the reader BACK to the vow-text of v. 31 — forcing the reader to reconstruct what "doing the vow" entailed.

Contrast with the immediate follow-up clause (v. 39b): - וְהִיא לֹא־יָדְעָה אִישׁ (WX-QATAL: waw + fronted pronoun + negative + qatal + noun) - The narrator DOES provide an interpretive gloss — but in SEXUAL/MARITAL TERMS (she had not known a man), not MORTALITY TERMS (she died, she was slain, her blood was poured out).

The narrator's chosen lexicon at v. 39 tracks the lifelong-virginity reading — the daughter's continuing living-state of unmarriedness as the outcome of her father's vow.

docs.title: Part 4: LXX/Greek Bridges (relevant to NT echoes and ancient reception)
G1334 — διηγέομαι (diegeomai) "recount, narrate"
docs.text:
• LXX Jdg 5:11 renders תנה with this verb
  • Heb 11:32 uses the cognate participle διηγούμενον ("recounting") of the author's own listing of the faith heroes — a verb-choice consciously evocative of Jdg 5:11 and (LXX-A presumably) Jdg 11:40's commemorative rite.

docs.title: G2422 — Ἰεφθάε (Iephthae) "Jephthah"
docs.text: • Proper noun in Heb 11:32. Included in the faith-roll without qualification or specific commendation in v. 32. The nine aorist verbs of vv. 33-34 that specify the faith-acts all map to military-deliverance semantic field.

docs.title: G3439 — μονογενής (monogenes) "only-begotten / only-one"
docs.text:
• LXX rendering of yachid at Jdg 11:34 (paired with ἀγαπητή) and at Ps 22:20, 25:16, 35:17
  • Heb 11:17 uses μονογενῆ of Isaac — echoing LXX Gen 22:2 vocabulary
  • John 1:14, 18; 3:16; 1 John 4:9 — Christological title for Jesus
  • The lexical thread runs: yachid Hebrew → ἀγαπητός/μονογενής LXX → Christological NT. Jdg 11:34's daughter is in this thread.

docs.title: G3646 — ὁλοκαύτωμα (holokautoma) "whole burnt offering"
docs.text:
• LXX rendering of olah (Lev 1 throughout); Heb 10:6, 8 quotes via Ps 40:6. English "holocaust" derives.
  • LXX Jdg 11:31 uses ὁλοκαύτωμα for olah (both A-text and B-text). The Greek translators chose the cultic-technical term, interpreting Jephthah's vow sacrificially.

docs.title: G2354 — θρηνέω (threneo) "lament, sing a dirge"
docs.text: • LXX Jdg 11:40's rendering of letannot. The Greek translators' interpretive commitment to the literal-sacrifice reading is evidenced by this verb-choice — but the verb is NOT cognate to Hebrew תנה; it is the Greek translator's interpretive paraphrase. The Hebrew verb itself (dis legomenon) is not lexically "lament"; the LXX-chosen θρηνεῖν is reading-dependent.

docs.title: Part 5: Synthesis — Lexical Verdict
docs.text:
The vow-utterance (Jdg 11:31): Jephthah's vow employs the Hiphil alah + olah collocation that every canonical parallel uses for ACTUAL burnt-offering presentation. He did NOT employ the Hannah-style natan + kol yemei chayyav dedication-formula, nor the Lev 27:2 yaphli neder + 'erekh person-valuation formula, nor the Exo 13:2 qaddesh + kol peter rechem firstborn-consecration formula. Lexically, the vow IS phrased sacrificially.

The vow-execution (Jdg 11:39): The narrator reports the fulfillment with bare עשׂה, deliberately avoiding every Hebrew sacrifice-verb he had available. Lexically, the execution is NOT reported sacrificially.

The gloss-clause (Jdg 11:39b): The narrator supplies ONE circumstantial clause — in sexual/marital terms ("and she had not known a man") — parallel to the daughter's own bethulim-mourning vocabulary of 11:37-38 and to the living-virginal-chastity formula of Gen 24:16 / Num 31:18 / 1 Kgs 1:4.

The annual-rite clause (Jdg 11:40): The narrator uses the dis legomenon תנה, which the ONLY other canonical occurrence (Jdg 5:11) governs toward "recount/commemorate." Grammar (ל of benefit, not עַל of object-of-mourning) confirms.

Resolution options: - A. Literal-sacrifice: Jephthah's vow is SACRIFICIALLY phrased (Hiphil alah + olah); the narrator's restraint at 11:39 is euphemistic avoidance of naming the sordid act; "lo yada ish" is a retrospective observation about her status as she died; tanah = lament. REQUIRES: (1) narrator chose plain asah to soften a sacrificial reality; (2) WX-QATAL read sequentially against its typical function (cf. Gen 24:16 reads stative); (3) tanah read against its Jdg 5:11 control. The reading fits the vow-utterance but strains every narrator-choice at the execution. - B. Lifelong-dedication / perpetual virginity: Jephthah's vow uses sacrificial vocabulary because either (a) he rashly meant it that way and was ignorant/illiterate of Lev 27:1-8 redemption, or (b) he was employing disjunctive-waw intending "dedicated OR sacrificed according to what emerged." Either way, the narrator's lexical restraint at 11:39, the WX-QATAL circumstantial, the bethulim-mourning register, and the tanah-commemorative rite all POINT to a living-dedication resolution — possibly mediated by a priestly valuation invoked tacitly (Targum's Phinehas) or by a lifelong-virginity consecration (Samuel/Nazarite parallel).

Both readings HAVE to absorb some friction with the text. The analysis agent will weigh which friction-set is smaller.
