docs.title: Did Jephthah Sacrifice His Daughter, or Dedicate Her to Perpetual Virginity?
Question
docs.text: Did Jephthah actually sacrifice his daughter as a burnt offering (Judges 11:29-40), or did he dedicate her to perpetual virginity / lifelong temple service? What does the Hebrew text, the grammar of the vow, and the surrounding biblical theology indicate?

docs.title: Summary Answer
docs.text:
The biblical evidence points with high confidence to a literal human sacrifice. Jephthah vowed in sacrificial language (Hiphil 'alah + 'olah), reacted to his daughter's appearance with the Hebrew mourning gestures for death (lo 'ukhal lashuv, rending garments), and executed his vow. The narrator's bare wayyaasat 11:39 is referential compression back to the vow-text of v.31, not lexical denial of sacrifice. The follow-up WX-QATAL clausewehi lo-yadah ish is attendant-state (state at the moment of fulfillment), not durative-subsequent (lifetime of celibacy). The LXX witness at 11:31 is uniformly conjunctive (καί or asyndeton; never disjunctive ἤ). Josephus and the Targum both read literal sacrifice. The act was a sinful, Torah-violating execution of a rash vow — neither divinely commanded nor divinely accepted (Jer 7:31), but sincerely and tragically carried through by a Spirit-empowered but morally entangled judge, which is the characteristic Judges-era pattern.

This reverses the v1 study's "modestly favors dedication" verdict because v1's two strongest legs collapse under closer grammatical scrutiny: (1) the WX-QATAL at 11:39b is circumstantial/attendant-state per the grammar-reference library's own dedicated entry, NOT durative subsequent-lifetime celibacy, and (2) the LXX at 11:31 never renders the waw disjunctively in either recension, contrary to the Clarke-Henry disjunctive-waw tradition.

docs.title: Key Verses
docs.text:
Jdg 11:30-31 "And Jephthah vowed a vow unto the LORD, and said, If thou shalt without fail deliver the children of Ammon into mine hands, Then it shall be, that whatsoever cometh forth of the doors of my house to meet me, when I return in peace from the children of Ammon, shall surely be the LORD'S, and I will offer it up for a burnt offering."

Jdg 11:35 "And it came to pass, when he saw her, that he rent his clothes, and said, Alas, my daughter! thou hast brought me very low, and thou art one of them that trouble me: for I have opened my mouth unto the LORD, and I cannot go back."

Jdg 11:39 "And it came to pass at the end of two months, that she returned unto her father, who did with her [according] to his vow which he had vowed: and she knew no man. And it was a custom in Israel,"

Jdg 11:40 "[That] the daughters of Israel went yearly to lament the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite four days in a year."

Gen 22:2 "And he said, Take now thy son, thine only [son] Isaac, whom thou lovest, and get thee into the land of Moriah; and offer him there for a burnt offering upon one of the mountains which I will tell thee of."

2 Kgs 3:27 "Then he took his eldest son that should have reigned in his stead, and offered him [for] a burnt offering upon the wall."

Jer 7:31 "And they have built the high places of Tophet, which [is] in the valley of the son of Hinnom, to burn their sons and their daughters in the fire; which I commanded [them] not, neither came it into my heart."

1 Sam 1:11 "And she vowed a vow, and said, O LORD of hosts... then I will give him unto the LORD all the days of his life, and there shall no rasor come upon his head."

Deut 12:31 "Thou shalt not do so unto the LORD thy God: for every abomination to the LORD, which he hateth, have they done unto their gods; for even their sons and their daughters they have burnt in the fire to their gods."

Heb 11:32-34 "And what shall I more say? for the time would fail me to tell of Gedeon, and of Barak, and of Samson, and of Jephthae... Who through faith subdued kingdoms, wrought righteousness... waxed valiant in fight, turned to flight the armies of the aliens."

docs.title: The lexical register of the vow forecloses a dedication reading
docs.text:
At the moment of the vow (Jdg 11:31), Jephthah had five Torah-authenticated vocabularies available for placing a person under a lifelong God-ward obligation. He used none of them.

(1) The Hannah-Levite dedication register — natan laYHWH kol yemei chayyav ("give to YHWH all the days of his life"). This is how Hannah vows Samuel (1 Sam 1:11) and how the Levites are said to be netunim netunim laYHWH (Num 3:9; 8:16, 19). The verb natan + the explicit duration clause kol yemei chayyav + (in Samuel's case) the Nazirite "no razor" marker form the paradigmatic living-dedication formula. Jephthah used neither the verb natan, nor the duration clause, nor any separation-marker.

(2) The Lev 27:1-8 redemption register — yaphli neder + beerkekha nephashot laYHWH("when a man shall make a singular vow, the persons shall be for the LORD by thy estimation"). This is the Torah's own answer to exactly the scenario Jephthah faced: a person is vowed to YHWH; the priest assigns a silver valuation (adult woman 20-60 = 30 shekels); the vow is discharged by payment; the person goes home. The library's word study on H6187'erekexplicitly notes: "the category Lev 27:1-8'erek` EXPLICITLY CONTEMPLATES THE SCENARIO OF JDG 11." Jephthah did not invoke it. He did not consult Phinehas the high priest (Jdg 20:27-28), who was alive and ministering at the ark in the same era.

(3) The Exodus 13:2 firstborn-consecration register — qaddesh li kol bekor (Piel qadash + peter rechem). Every firstborn was consecrated; every firstborn SON was then redeemed by a lamb or by five shekels (Exo 13:13; Num 18:15-16). Jephthah did not use the Piel qadash at all.

(4) The Nazirite register — yazir nezer laYHWH (Num 6:2), separation-by-vow for life-service. Samson (Jdg 13:5), Samuel (1 Sam 1:11), the Rechabites (Jer 35), and John the Baptist (Lk 1:15) are canonical instances. Jephthah did not use nazar (H5144) or any separation-vocabulary.

(5) The judicial-ban register (cherem) — charam + cherem. Israel at Hormah (Num 21:2); Jericho (Josh 6-7); Amalek (1 Sam 15). Cherem-dedication is non-redeemable per Lev 27:28-29 and applies to enemies of YHWH, not family members. Jephthah did not use this vocabulary either.

(6) The sacrificial-offering register (what he actually used) — Hiphil 'alah + 'olah ("cause to go up" + "burnt offering"). In the entire OT this collocation reports actual burnt offerings: Noah's animals post-flood (Gen 8:20), Abraham's attempted offering of Isaac and the ram-substitute (Gen 22:2, 13), the Leviticus ordinance (Lev 17:8), Mesha the Moabite's human child offering (2 Kgs 3:27), Solomon's thousand sacrifices (2 Chr 1:6). There is no canonical non-literal occurrence.

Jephthah's choice of vocabulary tells us what Jephthah intended to do if anyone came out of his doors: he would literally offer that one as a burnt offering. This was a rash, desperate battlefield vow, presumably expecting a slave or a servant or an animal to greet him. But the referent-class of the participle hayyotze was already skewed human (H3318 word study: zero animal referents; liqra'ti is a social-encounter idiom performed by persons). Jephthah had made an unwisely open-ended vow whose grammatical default was a human.

docs.title: The grammar of the vow does not admit a disjunctive-waw reading
docs.text:
Clarke (following rabbinic tradition) argued that wehayah laYHWH wehaalitihu olah can be translated "shall be to YHWH OR I will offer him up as a burnt offering" — with animal subjects taken as sacrifices and human subjects taken as dedications. The argument relies on Hebrew waw's polyfunctionality.

Three decisive data points against:

First, Hebrew has a dedicated disjunctive particle o (H176), and the author of Judges uses it at Jdg 11:34 — just three verses after the contested waw — to say "neither son OR daughter" (ben o-bat). The same author knew the disjunctive particle and chose not to use it in the vow.

Second, the LXX at 11:31 never renders the waw disjunctively. The A-text (Codex Alexandrinus, closer to Old Greek): καὶ ἔσται τῷ κυρίῳ καὶ ἀνοίσω αὐτὸν ὁλοκαύτωμα — a direct vote for conjunctive "and." The B-text (Codex Vaticanus, kaige-Theodotionic revision): καὶ ἔσται τῷ κυρίῳ· ἀνοίσω αὐτὸν ὁλοκαύτωμα — asyndeton, neither conjunctive nor disjunctive explicitly. Neither recension uses ἤ (the Greek disjunctive). Ancient Jewish translators had ἤ in their active vocabulary: it renders o at Exo 21:20 and Lev 5:1 and is used in countless disjunctive contexts throughout the LXX. Its absence at Jdg 11:31 in both recensions is substantial negative evidence: the earliest Jewish readers did not see disjunction in this verse.

Third, no canonical OT vow-apodosis chain of two weqatal clauses carries unambiguous disjunctive force between them. Jacob at Gen 28:20-22, Israel at Num 21:2, Hannah at 1 Sam 1:11, Absalom at 2 Sam 15:7-8 — every control case is conjunctive. The disjunctive reading at Jdg 11:31 would be a hapax, invented because the conjunctive reading is theologically uncomfortable.

docs.title: The WX-QATAL at 11:39b is state-at-moment, not lifetime-celibacy
docs.text:
v1's strongest syntactic argument was that wehi lo-yadah ish` ("and she knew no man") is a WX-QATAL disjunctive clause with durative force — a summary comment that she went on to live a life of celibacy.

The grammar-reference library's own dedicated entry on WX-QATAL (syntax-wx-qatal-circumstantial.md) explicitly concludes: "the clause is NOT a statement that her virginity continued after the vow's execution through a subsequent lifetime of celibacy (which would be the sequential reading); it is a background state-description framing the fulfillment of the vow... supplying attendant circumstance, not subsequent action."

Every WX-QATAL parallel supports the library's reading. Gen 3:1 wehanachash hayah 'arum — the serpent's state at scene-open, not a durative subsequent condition. Gen 13:2 we'Avram kaved me'od — Abram's state at the scene, not a lifetime prophecy. Gen 24:16 bethulah we'ish lo yedaah— Rebekah's state at the well, within days of marriage. 1 Kgs 1:4wehamelek lo yedaah — David's state at the introduction of Abishag, not a lifelong guarantee (Adonijah later claims Abishag, 1 Kgs 2:17-22). Jdg 14:4 we'aviv we'immo lo yadu` — Samson's parents' ignorance at the specific moment.

The WX-QATAL in Jdg 11:39b describes the daughter's state at the moment the vow was fulfilled. Her state was virginal — she had not known a man. This comports naturally with her two-month mourning over her bethulim (vv.37-38): she asked for two months to weep that she would die a virgin, and at the moment of the vow's fulfillment, she was in exactly that state. The narrator is closing the emotional arc of vv.37-38, not predicting an unrecorded subsequent lifetime.

docs.title: The narrator's wayyaas` is referential compression, not lexical denial
docs.text:
At 11:39a, the narrator reports the fulfillment with wayyaas lah et-nidro asher nadar("he did to her his vow which he had vowed"). The verb isasah` — the most colorless Hebrew verb — and the direct object is "his vow which he vowed," a cognate-accusative construction that binds the report tightly to the utterance of v.31.

v1 read this restraint as diagnostic silence: if Jephthah had sacrificed her, the narrator would have said so. But the argument proves too much. The narrator's restraint is not a choice between "sacrifice-vocabulary" and "neutral-vocabulary"; it is a choice between "describe the act directly" and "refer back to the vow-text." The narrator chose the latter. He wrote: he performed his vow. And his vow, in v.31, said Hiphil alah + olah. Reading v.39 through v.31's vow yields literal burnt offering.

The Judges-author is not squeamish. He narrates the king of Moab's bowels voiding as Ehud's dagger exits (Jdg 3:22). He narrates Jael's tent-peg through Sisera's temple (4:21). He narrates the Levite dismembering his concubine into twelve pieces (19:29). He narrates 25,100 Benjaminite corpses (20:35). This narrator can report atrocity. His choice at 11:39 is not delicate avoidance; it is a literary technique — compression via cross-reference — that forces the reader to reconstruct v.39 from v.31. Since v.31 is sacrificial, v.39 is sacrificial.

docs.title: Jephthah's own behavior comports with sacrifice, not dedication
docs.text:
When his daughter emerges, Jephthah rends his clothes (wayyiqra et-begadaw) — the Hebrew mourning gesture for DEATH (Gen 37:29, 34; 2 Sam 1:11; 3:31; 13:31; Job 1:20). He says she has "brought me very low" and "troubled me." He declarespatsiti pi el-YHWH welo 'ukhal lashuv` ("I have opened my mouth unto YHWH, and I cannot go back"). These are not the words of a father who has just recognized that his daughter will go live at the sanctuary. A man whose daughter was about to be dedicated to a honorable lifelong service would grieve — but he would grieve the loss of his line's posterity, not tear his clothes as for a funeral and declare himself unable to retract. Jephthah's words and gestures fit impending death.

The daughter's request to mourn her bethulim two months on the mountains fits the same reading: she is not asking for two months to prepare for sanctuary life (which would be unusual — Samuel went to Eli immediately upon weaning); she is asking for two months to weep that she will die a virgin, never married, never bearing sons or daughters, never continuing her father's line. For a young Israelite woman, this was the supreme loss, compounded by the fact that she is yechidah — her father's only child, whose death ends his posterity entirely.

docs.title: The LXX and Second Temple Jewish reception uniformly read literal sacrifice
docs.text:
The LXX renders Jdg 11:31 with conjunctive καί (A-text) or asyndeton (B-text) — never disjunctive ἤ. The LXX renders Jdg 11:34's yechidah with BOTH agapete AND monogenes — importing the full Akedah / Christological-only-begotten vocabulary into Jephthah's daughter. The LXX renders letannot at 11:40 with threnein ("lament") — committing to a funerary-memorial reading of the annual four-day rite.

Josephus (Antiquities 5.7.6) — first-century Jewish historian — states flatly: "he sacrificed his daughter as a burnt-offering, offering such an oblation as was neither conformable to the law nor acceptable to God, not weighing with himself what opinion the hearers would have of such a practice." Josephus reads literal sacrifice and declares it Torah-violating — exactly the position this analysis arrives at.

Targum Jonathan reads literal sacrifice and indicts Jephthah for failing to consult Phinehas the high priest, who would have redeemed the daughter via Lev 27:1-8 valuation. The Targum's halakhic commentary is exactly right on the law; Jephthah's failure to use the Torah off-ramp is real. But the commentary presupposes the sacrifice happened.

These are not authoritative for doctrine (per the principle_scripture_authority). They are interpretive witnesses, and their uniformity is a converging line of evidence that ancient Hebrew- and Greek-reading Jews heard the text as literal sacrifice. The dedication reading is a later exegetical move (fully developed by medieval rabbinic commentary; picked up in Christian apologetics by Adam Clarke), motivated by discomfort with the theological implications of the plain reading.

docs.title: Spirit-empowerment in Judges does not entail moral ratification
docs.text:
Clarke argued that the Spirit of YHWH (11:29) could not permit Jephthah to sacrifice his daughter — so the plain reading must be wrong. The argument is theologically appealing but fails the Judges-book test.

The same Spirit empowers Samson (Jdg 13:25; 14:6, 19; 15:14), who then marries a Philistine against parental and Torah counsel (14:1-3), consorts with a prostitute (16:1), and dies amid apostate Philistine idolatry. The same Spirit empowers Gideon (6:34), who then makes the gold ephod that "all Israel went thither a whoring after" (8:27). The same Spirit (in 1 Samuel) empowers Saul (1 Sam 10:10), whose subsequent trajectory is catastrophic disintegration (1 Sam 15; 19:23-24).

The pattern is unambiguous: in Judges-era Israel, Spirit-empowerment enables deliverance. It does not morally ratify subsequent conduct. The Spirit that empowered Jephthah to defeat Ammon (11:32-33) did not ratify the vow he uttered during the campaign. The Spirit was deliverance-Spirit, not sanctification-Spirit.

docs.title: Hebrews 11:32 is symmetrical on the vow question
docs.text:
The NT inclusion of Jephthah in the faith roll (Heb 11:32) is the final exegetical anchor for the dedication reading: how could a sacrificer be commended?

The answer is structural. Hebrews 11 commends discrete faith-acts, not whole biographies. The nine aorist verbs in 11:33-34 — katagonizomai (subdued), klino (turned to flight), etc. — all belong to the military-deliverance semantic field. Each maps to a specific deliverance-moment in the listed figures' lives: Gideon's Midian (Jdg 7), David's Philistines, Jephthah's Ammon (Jdg 11:32-33). None describes a vow, a priestly act, or a sacrifice.

And the faith roll's selectivity is everywhere. Samson included, but Heb 11 does not endorse his Philistine wife or his Gaza harlot visit. Gideon included, but Heb 11 does not endorse his gold ephod. David included (in v.32), but Heb 11 does not endorse his adultery and murder. Rahab included (v.31), but Heb 11 does not endorse her prior trade — only her faith-act of hiding the spies.

Heb 11:32 commends Jephthah's faith-driven deliverance of Israel from Ammon. It does not commend, endorse, or even comment on the vow or its execution. The vow's ethics must be evaluated on OT grounds — where the Torah's categorical prohibition of human sacrifice (Lev 18:21; 20:2-5; Deut 12:31; 18:10; Jer 7:31) is univocal and decisive.

docs.title: The Akedah template is inverted, not replicated
docs.text:
The narrative visibly echoes Genesis 22: the yachid child, the Hiphil alah + olah vocabulary, the high-place setting, the two-day / three-day / two-month travel interval before fulfillment, the obedient child. But the Akedah ends with a ram in the thicket and a voice from heaven: "lay not thine hand upon the lad" (Gen 22:12). Jdg 11 ends with no ram, no voice, no arrest.

Under the dedication reading, the narrator's silence on an arrest is a gap the reader fills with "tacit Lev 27 redemption." But the narrator of Judges elsewhere fills gaps when they matter: he tells us Samson's riddle, Gideon's fleece-signs, the Levite's reception at Jabesh-Gilead. If a Lev 27 redemption had happened, it would be narratively significant enough to mention. Its absence is not reticence; it is evidence that no arrest occurred.

The Jdg 11 narrative is Genesis 22 uninverted: a father who was NOT commanded by God made a vow he should not have made, and when the ram did not come, he went through with the knife. This is the Akedah turned to tragedy — which is the thematic logic of Judges as a whole: what Israel's fathers did under divine command (Abraham's near-sacrifice of Isaac, arrested by YHWH) becomes in Israel's disintegration what its judges do under the compulsion of their own rash oaths, unhindered because there is no king in Israel (Jdg 17:6; 21:25).

docs.title: Theological logic: Judges does not soften its judges
docs.text:
The book of Judges exists to tell Israel a story it found embarrassing. Ehud's fat-king-bowel-voiding is gratuitous detail. Barak needs Deborah to lead him. Gideon's Midian-victory leads immediately to ephod-idolatry. Jephthah's Ammon-victory leads immediately to his daughter's burning. Samson's Philistine victories come amid his moral and cultic chaos. The book's closing chapters (Jdg 17-21) describe Israel's Levite-concubine atrocity, the tribes' near-annihilation of Benjamin, and the mass kidnapping of virgins at Shiloh.

The book's refrain is clear: "In those days there was no king in Israel; every man did that which was right in his own eyes" (Jdg 17:6; 21:25). The refrain interprets the Jephthah episode exactly. Jephthah did what was right in his own eyes — made a rash vow, executed it with terrible consistency when no community override intervened (contrast 1 Sam 14:45 where Israel overrode Saul's oath to save Jonathan). The daughter paid the price. And the daughters of Israel, in their annual lament, preserved the memory as a warning.

To read Judges 11 as a story of lifelong dedication is to domesticate a text that Judges designed to be horrifying. The narrator wants us to feel the lo 'ukhal lashuv — Jephthah's tragic absolutism, his misreading of Torah, his failure to invoke the Lev 5 guilt-offering or the Lev 27 priestly redemption or the Ecc 5:6 error-confession. Judges wants us to see a Spirit-empowered judge whose faith delivered Israel in battle but whose theological formation was so rudimentary that he confused a neder with a cherem and killed his own daughter. That is the book Judges wrote. The dedication reading asks us to read a different book.

docs.title: Word Studies
docs.text:
The canonical library entries integrated into this analysis: - H5087 nadar / H5088 neder — confirm Jephthah's vow is formally a neder, hence Lev 27:1-8 redeemable, NOT Lev 27:28-29 cherem non-redeemable. - H5927 alah / H5930olah — confirm Jephthah's Hiphil + olah collocation is the dedicated sacrificial-register formula; every OT occurrence reports literal burnt offering. - H2076 zabach — notably absent at 11:39, but ALSO absent at 2 Kgs 3:27's Mesha sacrifice (the closest parallel). zabach is zevach-class, not olah-class, so its absence is expected for both Jephthah and Mesha. - H2763 charam / H2764 cherem — confirm the legal category distinction between Jephthah's neder and the judicial cherem category. - H1330 bethulah / H1331 bethulim — confirm bethulim is abstract plural of condition; the mourning-object is the virginity-state. Library entry does not force the "living woman" constraint. - H3045 yada — confirms 15 sexual-euphemism occurrences; feminine lo yadah ishis always about living women AT THE MOMENT, not lifetime-celibacy predictions. - **H3173 yachid** — Jdg 11:34 is the only OT verse where LXX uses BOTHagapeteANDmonogenes; full Akedah intertext. - **H6187erek — confirms the Torah redemption off-ramp Jephthah did not invoke (adult female 20-60 = 30 shekels). - H6942 qadash — absent from Jdg 11; Jephthah did not use the consecration vocabulary. - H5414 natan — absent from Jdg 11; Jephthah did not use Hannah's dedication-verb. - H3318 yatsa — hayyotze has zero animal referents in OT; the participle already presupposed a human. - H8567 tanah — dis legomenon; LXX splits between διηγέομαι (5:11) and θρηνεῖν (11:40), the latter committing to a funerary reading. - H5144 nazar** — absent from Jdg 11; Jephthah did not use Nazirite vocabulary.

Grammar-reference library entries: - passages/jdg-11-30-31.md — full grammatical analysis of the vow - passages/jdg-11-37-40.md — analysis of daughter's response and fulfillment - hebrew/syntax-conditional-vow.md — im-protasis / weqatal apodosis template - hebrew/syntax-waw-conjunction.md — waw polyfunctionality; default conjunctive - hebrew/weqatal-apodosis-vow.md — foreground weqatal in vow apodoses - hebrew/syntax-wx-qatal-circumstantial.md — the decisive grammar entry confirming 11:39b is attendant-state, not durative-subsequent - greek/lxx-disjunctive-waw-vows.md — the decisive LXX entry documenting that neither recension uses ἤ at 11:31

docs.title: The strongest residual objection: narrator's lexical restraint at 11:39
docs.text:
The narrator's choice of bare wayyaas` over every available sacrifice-verb remains a real phenomenon. It is the single strongest argument for the dedication reading.

The counter-explanation — referential compression back to v.31 — is plausible but not watertight. It is possible that the narrator shrinks from naming the horrific act and trusts the reader to reconstruct it. It is also possible that the narrator is hinting at a different resolution.

Two considerations weight toward the referential-compression reading: (1) the narrator does supply a follow-up gloss in v.39b, but that gloss is in sexual/marital terms ("she knew no man"), not in life-service terms ("and she ministered before YHWH" or "and she was given to the sanctuary"). If the narrator wanted to signal dedication, v.39b was the obvious place. (2) The narrator continues in v.39c with wattehi choq beYisra'el ("and it became a custom in Israel") — pointing forward to the annual four-day rite, which the LXX committed to reading as lament. The whole v.39-40 unit points toward funerary memorial, not toward lifelong dedicated service.

But the objection is real. A strict weighing places this as the residual difficulty the literal-sacrifice reading must absorb. Absorbed, but honestly noted.

docs.title: The letannot dis legomenon ambiguity
docs.text:
The semantic range of H8567 in its only two canonical occurrences (Jdg 5:11 celebrative; Jdg 11:40 contested) does not fully settle the question. The LXX's choice of θρηνεῖν at 11:40 commits to lament, but LXX-translators were readers, not linguistic oracles. Modern translations split (KJV "lament"; NASB "commemorate"; NET "remember"; ESV "lament"). This is the second strongest residual uncertainty.

The weight of the full passage (vv.39-40 together) and the Near Eastern parallel to young-woman memorial rites (Tammuz/Adonis-type lamentations, which the Israelite prophets later denounce) tip the reading toward lament. But "commemorate" cannot be entirely ruled out, and a reading in which the four-day rite mingled remembrance with lament (as many Near Eastern mourning rites do) is lexically adequate to both readings.

docs.title: The Akedah intertext could be read either way
docs.text:
The yechidah echo (Gen 22:2 + Jdg 11:34) is so tight that the narrator is clearly cueing Abraham's Isaac episode. Under the dedication reading, the intertext points to arrest-by-substitution (even if the substitute is unnamed). Under the sacrifice reading, the intertext points to an un-arrested tragic inverse.

This is genuinely ambiguous as intertextual rhetoric. But the absence of any narrated arrest, ram, or angel-from-heaven tips the reading toward the tragic-inverse interpretation. A narrator who wanted us to see a substitution would supply the substitute. The narrator of Gen 22 supplies the ram. The narrator of Jdg 11 supplies only the wayyaas`.

docs.title: Jephthah's Torah-literacy raises the theological cost of the literal reading
docs.text:
Jephthah's diplomatic speech (Jdg 11:12-28) demonstrates accurate knowledge of Num 20-21 and Deut 2. If he knew Pentateuchal narrative, he knew Pentateuchal law — Deut 12:31 explicitly forbidding applying Canaanite child-immolation to YHWH, Lev 18:21 and 20:2-5 capital-offense prohibitions, Lev 5:4-5 rash-oath atonement, Lev 27:1-8 vow redemption.

Under the literal reading, this makes Jephthah a Torah-literate knowing violator — which is theologically uncomfortable. It is tempting to rescue him by reading his act as dedication, not sacrifice. But the rescue fails on the linguistic evidence. What the Torah-literacy actually shows is the characteristic Judges-era tragedy: knowing the law does not save you from the cultural and personal forces driving disobedience. Jephthah knew and did otherwise. That is the theology of Judges — and Romans 2:17-24 — applied in narrative form.

docs.title: Why the dedication reading has attracted serious scholars
docs.text:
Clarke, Henry (partially), and a long rabbinic tradition opted for dedication not out of bad faith but out of three genuine observations: (1) the narrator's unusual restraint at 11:39; (2) the bethulim / lo-yadah ish` clustering which superficially reads like a living-virgin formula; (3) the theological discomfort of a Spirit-empowered judge, member of Heb 11's faith roll, ending the narrative as a child-sacrificer.

These are real observations. The response to each: (1) referential compression is a literary technique the Judges-author demonstrably uses, and 11:39b's gloss is state-at-moment, not lifetime-celibacy; (2) both bethulim and the WX-QATAL clause comport with "she died a virgin" just as naturally as with "she lived on a virgin" — and the Hannah / Samuel / Anna living-dedication register would have required different vocabulary; (3) Judges 11 ends Jephthah's narrative in tragedy because Judges ends every judge's narrative in some shade of tragedy. Samson in the rubble of Dagon's temple amid Philistine corpses. Gideon surrounded by the gold-ephod he made. Deborah-Barak's victory followed by twenty years of peace and then back into apostasy. Jephthah is the rule, not the exception.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
The Bible teaches that Jephthah literally sacrificed his daughter as a burnt offering in fulfillment of a rash vow he should never have made. This sacrifice was sinful, Torah-violating, and divinely unaccepted. It was carried out by a Spirit-empowered but morally entangled judge in a book whose explicit theological purpose is to narrate Israel's moral and religious disintegration between Joshua and Samuel.

Established with high confidence: 1. The vow of Jdg 11:31 used the dedicated sacrificial collocation Hiphil alah + olah, which in the OT corpus has no non-literal occurrence. 2. The vow did not use any of five Torah-authenticated dedication/redemption/consecration vocabularies. 3. The LXX at 11:31 never renders the waw disjunctively in either recension. 4. The WX-QATAL at 11:39b is attendant-state, per the grammar-reference library's own verdict. 5. Jephthah's mourning gestures at 11:35 are funerary, not dedicatory. 6. The narrator's wayyaasat 11:39 is referential compression pointing back to v.31's sacrificial vocabulary. 7. Josephus, Targum Jonathan, Edersheim, and the LXX all read literal sacrifice; the dedication reading is a later, exegetically-motivated move. 8. Spirit-empowerment in Judges is deliverance-enabler, not moral ratifier. 9. Heb 11:32 commends discrete faith-acts (Ammonite military victory for Jephthah), not whole biographies. 10. The Torah's categorical prohibition of human sacrifice (Lev 18:21; 20:2-5; Deut 12:31; 18:10; Jer 7:31) classifies Jephthah's act — if literal — as sin. Jer 7:31'swelo altaal-libbi` precludes divine acceptance.

Remaining uncertainties: 1. The full semantic range of letannot in Jdg 11:40 remains underdetermined by its dis legomenon status. LXX θρηνεῖν weights lament; modern translations split. 2. The precise degree of Jephthah's Torah-literacy at the moment of vow-utterance vs. at the moment of execution is not fully recoverable. He may have uttered the vow without fully appreciating its implications, and then been psychologically unable to retract. 3. Whether Phinehas was genuinely absent from Jephthah's circle or whether Jephthah chose not to consult him is underdetermined. The narrative simply does not record consultation.

Theological implications:

On vows and rash oaths: Torah provides multiple off-ramps for unwise oaths (Lev 5:4-5 guilt-offering; Lev 27:1-8 redemption; Num 30 annulment mechanics). These were not invoked. Ecc 5:2-6 and Mt 5:33-37 and Jas 5:12 collectively condemn the vow-absolutism that drove Jephthah's tragedy: "Swear not at all... let your communication be yea, yea; nay, nay" (Mt 5:37).

On God's tolerance of flawed judges: The Spirit of YHWH empowered deliverance through Samson (who married pagans and visited prostitutes), Gideon (who made an idol-ephod), and Jephthah (who sacrificed his daughter). God's use of a person does not equal God's endorsement of all that person does. The faith roll in Heb 11 commends discrete faith-acts; it does not airbrush moral failures.

On the theology of the Judges era: The book portrays Israel's moral collapse between Joshua and Samuel. The refrain "there was no king in Israel; every man did that which was right in his own eyes" (Jdg 17:6; 21:25) interprets the Jephthah episode exactly. Without effective kingship or priestly leadership (where is Phinehas?), even Spirit-empowered deliverers can misread Torah catastrophically.

On what the text is for: Judges 11:29-40 exists as a warning, not as a model. The annual four-day rite of bnot Yisra'el mourning Jephthah's daughter (11:40) is preserved in the canon so that Israel would remember not to repeat the folly. The text is not hagiography; it is cautionary tragedy.

Practical/pastoral application:

On rash vows: Do not make them. Jesus cuts vow-making at the root (Mt 5:34-37). If you have made one and it would require sin to fulfill, Torah itself (via Lev 5:4-5 and the NT's broader ethic of seeking reconciliation over ritual absolutism) provides off-ramps. Breaking an unwise oath is far less serious than executing a sinful one.

On discerning Spirit-empowerment: The Spirit equips for service; the Spirit does not overwrite moral judgment. A Spirit-empowered person can still sin gravely in the very same season as their anointing. The presence of power is not the presence of righteousness.

On reading Scripture honestly: The temptation to domesticate difficult texts by reading them as metaphor or dedication, when the plain sense is tragic and morally scandalous, should be resisted. The canon contains what it contains precisely because the Spirit intends these stories to shape our moral imagination in specific ways. Flattening Jephthah into a lifelong-dedication patron saint empties the story of its pastoral force.

On the cost of cultural assimilation: Jephthah, fighting the Ammonites, reproduced in his daughter the exact kind of sacrifice the Ammonites themselves practiced (Molech worship — Lev 18:21; Jer 32:35). Living among paganism and opposing it in battle does not immunize against doing its deeds. Theological vigilance in the forms of one's own worship is required even in the moment of victory over pagan neighbors.

docs.title: Bible Study: Did Jephthah Actually Sacrifice His Daughter, or Dedicate Her to Perpetual Virginity?
Question
docs.text: Did Jephthah actually sacrifice his daughter as a burnt offering (Judges 11:29-40), or did he dedicate her to perpetual virginity / lifelong temple service? What does the Hebrew text, the grammar of the vow, and the surrounding biblical theology indicate?

docs.title: Prior Research Summary
docs.text:
v1 prior iteration (jephthah-daughter-sacrifice-v1, 2026-04-16) concluded the weight of biblical evidence modestly favors the perpetual-virginity / lifelong-dedication reading over literal burnt-offering. v1's five main legs:

  1. Narrator's lexical restraint at the climax (Jdg 11:39) — colorless wayyaas lah et-nidroavoids every available sacrifice verb (zabach,shachat,saraph,alahHiphil,heevir baesh) — contrast 2 Kgs 3:27, 16:3, 21:6, 23:10, Jer 19:5, Ezek 23:37-39, Gen 22:10.
  2. lo-yadah ish` (11:39) as WX-QATAL stative/durative — every other canonical occurrence describes living women's chastity (Gen 24:16; Num 31:17-18, 35; Jdg 21:11-12; 1 Kgs 1:4).
  3. bethulim (H1331) mourning of virginity-state, not impending death — no OT parallel for anticipatory 2-month self-mourning (cf. Gen 50:10 Jacob 7d; Num 20:29 Aaron 30d).
  4. letannot (H8567) Piel in Jdg 11:40 — dis legomenon; Jdg 5:11 control = "recount/celebrate" (yetannu tzidqot YHWH).
  5. Torah's categorical prohibition + Jer 7:31 divine disavowal forecloses divinely-accepted sacrifice.

v1 conceded default syntax of Jdg 11:31 (wehayah laYHWH wehaalitihu olah) is conjunctive weqatal;alahHiphil +olahis a technical cultic collocation (Gen 8:20; 22:13); Jephthah'slo ukhal lashuv` (11:35) indicates he understood the vow as irrevocable.

v1 gaps to close in v2: (a) Hebrew-grammar argument lacked canonical grammar-reference entries on WX-QATAL disjunctive/circumstantial and weqatal-apodosis; (b) LXX cross-check on 11:31, 39, 40 not done systematically; (c) Targum Jonathan/Phinehas-redemption gloss not addressed; (d) letannot vs Jdg 5:11 LXX verb-choice not tested; (e) Jephthah Torah-literacy argument (Jdg 11:12-28 vs Num 20-21) not surfaced; (f) Heb 11:32-34 Greek aorist verbs not parsed and matched to specific Jephthah acts.

External corpus signal (from 00-references.md): - EGW silent on the pericope — doctrine rests on Scripture alone (principle_scripture_authority). - Clarke Commentary (28187, 28216, 28230, 28231, 28288): disjunctive-waw vow reading; moral-impossibility argument; Spirit-of-YHWH argument; Targum Jonathan gloss on 11:39 — "Jephthah did not consult Phinehas... who would have redeemed her with money" (literal-sacrifice view but Torah-violation framing). - Henry Commentary (9409, 9411, 9412, 9414, 9976): surveys both; objects "no law or custom intimated celibacy as religion"; notes Num 30:5 father's annulment; connects 1 Sam 1:11 Hannah/Samuel as controlling parallel. - Edersheim BHOTV3 140.1, 141.1: literal sacrifice; "praise" (not "lament") for letannot; rejects the "rough-times Torah-ignorant Jephthah" defense since Jdg 11:15-27 demonstrates Pentateuchal literacy. - Josephus Ant. 5.7.6, 5.7.10: literal sacrifice; "neither conformable to the law nor acceptable to God"; paraphrases vow as "what living creature soever" (supports disjunctive-vow reading that vow anticipated any creature, not specifically a human). - SU silent; 1 Meqabyan 4:30 — general Satanic condemnation of child sacrifice (minor lead, skip). - Sirach 18:22 — intertestamental vow-ethic pushed vow-payment absolutism (contextualizes Josephus' literal reading).

These must be generated by /word-study and /grammar-study agents BEFORE the research agent runs. Each is critical to the question.

Word studies to generate:

  • H3173 yachid — "only child" in 11:34 uven yechidah — loaded Akedah echo (Gen 22:2 et-yechidkha); 12 total OT occurrences; library has no entry. → /word-study H3173
  • **H6187 erek** — "valuation" / redemption-estimate; Lev 27:2-8 redemption-schedule noun; 35 occurrences; necessary for Targum-Jonathan/Phinehas claim that daughter was legally redeemable. →/word-study H6187`
  • H6942 qadash — "to be holy/consecrated"; if dedication reading holds, this verb-group must be checked against vowing-persons precedents (Exo 13:2 firstborn-consecration; Lev 27:14-25). → /word-study H6942
  • H5414 natan — "to give/assign"; 1 Sam 1:11 unetattiw laYHWH kol yemei chayyav — Hannah's parallel vow-verb. Jephthah uses wehayah laYHWH (be to YHWH) — contrast with Hannah's explicit natan-verb is a key lexical datum. → /word-study H5414
  • H3318 yatsa — "go out"; Jdg 11:31 participle hayyotze — masc-sing generic participle, widely debated (any-creature vs human-only). Verify via distribution of masc-sing hayyotze referent: animal (Gen 9:10, 14:17), human (Gen 17:6), mixed (Lev 25:28). → /word-study H3318

Grammar references to generate:

  • WX-QATAL circumstantial/stative clause after wayyiqtol — we + subject + QATAL disjunctive clause; v1's leg 2 relies on this construction giving durative/state force to lo-yadah ish(11:39); library has no entry; existing passage entryjdg-11-37-40.mdmentions it but no concept file. →/grammar-study mode2 hebrew WX-QATAL circumstantial clause (subject-fronted disjunctive qatal)`
  • LXX εἰ μή / ἤ disjunctive rendering of Hebrew waw — does LXX Jdg 11:31 A-text vs B-text render we-haalitihu olahwith καί (conjunctive) or ἤ (disjunctive)? This is the earliest pre-Christian Jewish reading; any LXX disjunctive is positive evidence for Clarke's reading. →/grammar-study mode2 greek LXX disjunctive rendering of Hebrew waw in vows`
  • **Cognate-accusative construction wayyaas... et-nidro asher nadar** — Jdg 11:39b; existingjdg-11-37-40.mdmentions this; if no concept-file, generate. →/grammar-study mode2 hebrew cognate-accusative construction` (skip if jdg-11-37-40 covers adequately)

Belong to priests — Num 18:14. Rash vows: Jephthah Jdg 11:29-40; Israelites vs Benjamites Jdg 21:5-6; 20:21. Forbidden — Deut 23:18. Minor — Mrk 7:11-13. Unclassified — Gen 28:20-21; 31:13; Lev 5:4-13; 22:18-25; Num 15:2-16; 30:1-16; Deut 23:18, 21-23; Job 22:27; Ps 22:25; 50:14-15; 56:12; 61:5, 8; 65:1; 66:13-14; 76:11; 116:14-19; Prov 20:25; Eccl 5:4-6; Jon 2:9; Nah 1:15. Instances: Jacob Gen 28:20-22; Micah's mother Jdg 17:2-3; Hannah 1 Sam 1:11 w/ 1:27-28; Elkanah 1 Sam 1:21; Absalom 2 Sam 15:7-8; Job Job 31:1; David Ps 132:2; Ananias/Sapphira Acts 5:1-11; Jews vs Paul Acts 23:12-15.

Figurative: ekklesia Isa 62:5; Jer 14:17; 31:4, 13; 2 Cor 11:2; purity 1 Cor 7:25, 37; Rev 14:4.

docs.title: Verse References (organized by research domain)
docs.text:
Primary narrative (Jdg 11): - Jdg 11:1-3 — Jephthah's origin - Jdg 11:12-28 — diplomatic speech (Torah-literacy test) - Jdg 11:29 — Spirit of YHWH - Jdg 11:30-31 — vow - Jdg 11:32-33 — victory - Jdg 11:34 — daughter emerges; yechidah echo - Jdg 11:35 — Jephthah's tearing of clothes; lo ukhal lashuv - Jdg 11:36 — daughter's consent - Jdg 11:37-38 — bethulim mourning, two months on mountains - Jdg 11:39 — fulfillment; lo-yadah ish; "custom in Israel" - Jdg 11:40 — annual four-dayletannot` rite - Jdg 12:1, 4, 7 — Jephthah's continued judgeship - Jdg 20:27-28 — Phinehas at Ark (era-check)

Vow law (Pentateuch): - Lev 5:4-5 — guilt-offering for rash oath (off-ramp #1) - Lev 7:16-18 — freewill/vow timing - Lev 22:18-25 — unblemished vow-offering - Lev 23:37-38 — vow-offering regulation - Lev 27:1-13 — redemption-schedule for vowed persons (off-ramp #2, KEY) - Lev 27:14-15, 16-25 — real-estate/land redemption - Lev 27:26-27 — firstborn exemption - Lev 27:28-29 — cherem non-redeemable / death penalty (contrast with neder) - Num 6:1-21 — Nazarite living-dedication - Num 15:2-16 — vow/freewill offering - Num 18:14 — cherem belongs to priests - Num 30:1-16 — father's/husband's annulment right - Num 21:2 — Israel at Hormah vow (weqatal apodosis parallel) - Deut 12:6, 11, 17-18, 26 — vows at central sanctuary - Deut 23:18, 21-23 — vow regulations

Pentateuchal prohibitions on human sacrifice: - Lev 18:21 — Molech prohibition - Lev 20:2-5 — Molech death penalty - Deut 12:29-31 — "every abomination... their sons and their daughters... in the fire" - Deut 18:9-12 — pass-through-fire with divination-abominations list

Later prophetic/historical condemnations: - 2 Kgs 3:27 — Mesha sacrifices son wayyaalehu olah— contrast with Jdg 11:39's silence - 2 Kgs 16:3 — Ahazheevir baesh - 2 Kgs 17:17 — Israelites generally - 2 Kgs 21:6 — Manasseh - 2 Kgs 23:10 — Josiah defiles Tophet - Jer 7:31 — asher lo-tzivviti velo alta al-libbi (divine disavowal) - Jer 19:5 — lisroph... olot labaal` - Jer 32:35 — Hinnom - Ezek 16:20-21 — "thou hast slain my children" - Ezek 20:26, 31 — "pass through fire" - Ezek 23:37-39 — sacrificed "to be devoured" - Ps 106:37-38 — sacrificed to demons - Isa 57:5 — slaying children in valleys - Mic 6:7 — rhetorical "shall I give my firstborn?"

Parallel vows and parallel vow-syntax: - Gen 8:20; 22:2, 13 — Hiphil + olah cultic collocation - Gen 22:2 — yechidkha Akedah echo - Gen 28:20-22 — Jacob vow (wehayah... layhwh parallel apodosis) - Num 21:1-3 — Israel's Hormah vow (conditional im + weqatal apodosis) - 1 Sam 1:11 — Hannah/Samuel (unetattiw laYHWH kol yemei chayyav — controlling parallel for LIFELONG-dedication) - 1 Sam 1:22, 24-28 — Samuel delivered and left at tabernacle - 1 Sam 2:22 — women serving at tent of meeting door - Exo 38:8 — women serving at tent of meeting door - 2 Sam 15:7-8 — Absalom's vow - 1 Kgs 1:4 — Abishag lifelong virginal attachment (lo yedaah`) - Eccl 5:4-6 — vow-payment ethic with "error" off-ramp

Parallel women's virginity descriptions (for lo-yadah ish`): - Gen 19:8 — Lot's daughters (living) - Gen 24:16 — Rebekah (living) - Num 31:17-18, 35 — preserved Midianite virgins (living) - Jdg 19:25; 21:11-12 — preserved Jabesh-Gilead virgins (living) - 1 Kgs 1:4 — Abishag (living) - Lev 21:13-14 — priest's marriage to virgin (living) - Deut 22:14-20 — virginity-proof (living)

Spirit-inhabitation + moral failure (Judges era controls): - Jdg 14:6, 19; 15:14 — Samson Spirit-empowerment - Jdg 14:1-3 — Samson Philistine wife - Jdg 16:1 — Samson prostitute - Jdg 6:34 — Gideon Spirit - Jdg 8:27 — Gideon ephod-idolatry - 1 Sam 10:10; 19:23-24 — Saul Spirit + disintegration

Community-override controls (annulment): - 1 Sam 14:24-45 — Saul's rash oath overridden for Jonathan - Num 30:3-5 — father's annulment of daughter's vow

letannot (H8567) Piel occurrences: - Jdg 5:11 — yetannu tzidqot YHWH (control — "recount/celebrate") - Jdg 11:40 — annual four-day rite (dis legomenon)

NT context: - Heb 11:32-34 — Jephthah in faith-roll - Acts 23:12-15 — rash oath parallel - Mat 5:33-37; Jas 5:12 — oath ethics - Luk 2:36-37 — Anna dedicated widow (living-virgin-like precedent) - Mrk 7:11-13 — Corban vow-abuse

docs.title: Focus Areas
docs.text:
1. Grammatical spine of Jdg 11:30-31 — default-conjunctive vs. disjunctive waw.
  2. WHAT: Determine whether wehayah laYHWH wehaalitihu olah` grammatically permits, favors, or forbids a disjunctive reading ("shall be to YHWH OR I will offer up as burnt offering").
  3. WHY: This is THE hinge. Default weqatal-chain is conjunctive (v1 conceded). Clarke/Henry argue disjunctive is admissible given waw's polyfunctionality and the unique vow-context.

  4. HOW: READ $GRAMMAR_REF/hebrew/syntax-conditional-vow.md, $GRAMMAR_REF/hebrew/syntax-waw-conjunction.md, $GRAMMAR_REF/hebrew/weqatal-apodosis-vow.md, $GRAMMAR_REF/passages/jdg-11-30-31.md. Parse all OT wehayah + X + we-QATAL vow/conditional constructions: Gen 28:20-22 (Jacob), Num 21:2 (Hormah), 1 Sam 1:11 (Hannah), 2 Sam 15:7-8 (Absalom). Tabulate whether any unambiguously carries disjunctive force. Scrutinize v1's Exo 21:15 citation for noun-level vs clausal disjunction. Run semantic_grammar.py "disjunctive waw" --hebrew and semantic_grammar.py "weqatal apodosis vow" --hebrew.

  5. Lexical restraint in Jdg 11:39 — what the narrator didn't say.

  6. WHAT: Establish that the narrator in 11:39 (wayyaas lah et-nidro asher nadar`) systematically avoids every Hebrew sacrifice-verb available to him.
  7. WHY: v1 leg 1 is strongest; in every parallel reported-killing (2 Kgs 3:27 Mesha wayyaalehu olah`; 2 Kgs 16:3; 21:6; 23:10; Jer 19:5; Gen 22:10), the Hebrew narrator names the act. Silence here is diagnostic.

  8. HOW: READ library entries for H5927 alah, H5930olah, H2076 zabach, H2763 charam. Run search_strongs.py --verses H5927 "offered" and --verses H2076 to confirm these verbs are available in narrative-reporting mode. Retrieve full text of 2 Kgs 3:27; 2 Kgs 16:3; 21:6; 23:10; Jer 7:31; 19:5; Ezek 16:20-21; 23:37-39; Ps 106:37-38; Gen 22:10. Tabulate the sacrifice-verb in each — contrast with 11:39's wayyaas`.

  9. lo-yadah ish` as WX-QATAL circumstantial — durative chastity clause.

  10. WHAT: Determine whether the subject-fronted we-hi lo yadah ish` is (a) summary of a completed event (she died virgin) or (b) durative/circumstantial clause describing an ongoing state.
  11. WHY: v1 leg 2. WX-QATAL after wayyiqtol is typically circumstantial/stative; every OT parallel construction describes LIVING women's virginal state.

  12. HOW: READ $GRAMMAR_REF/passages/jdg-11-37-40.md. GENERATE grammar-reference entry for WX-QATAL circumstantial clause. Parse all parallel clauses: Gen 24:16 (Rebekah — explicit bethulah we-ish lo yedaah); Num 31:17-18, 35; Jdg 21:11-12; 1 Kgs 1:4 Abishag (we-hamelek lo yedaah). Retrieve each and run hebrew_parser.py. Note whether any describes a deceased woman.

  13. bethulim (H1331) — abstract plural "virginity-state."

  14. WHAT: Determine whether the daughter mourned (a) impending death-as-a-virgin or (b) perpetual-virginity state itself.
  15. WHY: v1 leg 3. No OT parallel for anticipatory 2-month self-mourning of one's own death; bethulim elsewhere refers to living women's virginity-state as a legal/social condition.

  16. HOW: READ $WORD_STUDIES/H1330-bthuwlah.md, $WORD_STUDIES/H1331-bthuwliym.md. Retrieve all bethulim occurrences: Lev 21:13; Deut 22:14-20; Ezek 23:3, 8 (and verify completeness via search_strongs.py --verses H1331). Compare mourning-duration norms: Jacob 7 days (Gen 50:10); Aaron 30 days (Num 20:29); Moses 30 days (Deut 34:8) — ALL post-mortem. Anticipatory 2-month self-mourning is categorically unusual. Analyze daughter's geographical action — "on the mountains" — against Lam 1:4; 2:10 virgin-mourning imagery.

  17. letannot (H8567) Piel — dis legomenon meaning.

  18. WHAT: Determine whether the annual 4-day rite in 11:40 "laments" (KJV) or "recounts/celebrates" (NASB/NET, Edersheim, Clarke).
  19. WHY: v1 leg 4; KJV's "lament" presupposes literal-sacrifice reading. The ONLY other OT occurrence (Jdg 5:11 yetannu tzidqot YHWH) is celebrative.

  20. HOW: READ $WORD_STUDIES/H8567-tanah.md. GENERATE LXX-cross-check grammar-reference entry. Retrieve LXX A-text and B-text of Jdg 5:11 and Jdg 11:40 — does LXX use διηγέομαι (G1334, "recount") in both, or θρηνέω (lament) in 11:40? This is the earliest pre-Christian Jewish reading. Also check translation-tradition spread: KJV, NASB, NET, ESV, LXX-based renderings.

  21. Mosaic vow-law framework — neder vs cherem, and the redemption off-ramp.

  22. WHAT: Establish whether Jephthah's neder legally admitted monetary redemption (Lev 27:1-8) and whether private neder can cross into cherem (Lev 27:28-29).
  23. WHY: v1 leg 5; Targum-Jonathan gloss indicts Jephthah for not consulting Phinehas who would have applied Lev 27:1-8.

  24. HOW: READ library entries for H5087, H5088, H2763, H2764. GENERATE /word-study H6187 for the redemption-valuation noun. Parse Lev 27:1-8 (daughter's age-bracket; female 5-20 yrs = 10 shekels; 20-60 yrs = 30 shekels). Parse Lev 27:26-29 and determine whether the two categories (neder vs cherem) are formally distinct. Retrieve Lev 5:4-5 guilt-offering for rash oath (off-ramp #1). Retrieve Num 30:1-5 father's annulment rights (off-ramp #3). Run semantic_grammar.py for "vow redemption" if relevant.

  25. Torah's categorical prohibition + divine disavowal (Jer 7:31).

  26. WHAT: Establish whether Torah's prohibition of human sacrifice is categorical (not merely against Molech-specific acts) and whether YHWH could accept the act.
  27. WHY: v1 leg 6; Jer 7:31's asher lo-tzivviti velo alta al-libbi forecloses divine acceptance regardless of reading.

  28. HOW: Retrieve full text of Lev 18:21; 20:2-5; Deut 12:29-31; 18:9-12; Jer 7:31 (parse Hebrew); 19:5; 32:35; 2 Kgs 16:3; 21:6; 23:10; Ezek 16:20-21; 20:26, 31; 23:37-39; Ps 106:37-38; Isa 57:5; Mic 6:7. Run concept_context.py on Jer 7:31 scope=book. Critical observation: even literal-sacrifice readings (Josephus, Targum, Edersheim) concede Torah-violation — which neutralizes any "Heb 11:32 validates the vow" inference.

  29. Hannah/Samuel structural parallel (1 Sam 1:11).

  30. WHAT: Test whether Hannah's vow (unetattiw laYHWH kol yemei chayyav) is the controlling biblical parallel for "be to YHWH" dedication.
  31. WHY: Hannah uses the same laYHWH + life-dedication pattern; Samuel LIVED and served.

  32. HOW: READ $WORD_STUDIES/H5087-nadar.md, H5088-neder.md. GENERATE /word-study H5414 natan (Hannah's verb differs from Jephthah's wehayah). Retrieve 1 Sam 1:11, 22-28; 2:11, 18; Num 6:1-21 Nazarite law. Compare syntax: Hannah natan + kol yemei chayyav (life-long); Jephthah wehayah laYHWH (plain dative). Is the absence of kol yemei chayyav in Jdg 11:31 a lexical problem for the dedication-reading?

  33. Spirit-of-YHWH + subsequent sinful acts (Judges control).

  34. WHAT: Determine whether Jdg 11:29 Spirit-inhabitation implies divine ratification of Jephthah's subsequent vow/act.
  35. WHY: Clarke's third argument (Spirit "could not permit him to imbrue his hands"). Must be tested against Judges-era controls — does Spirit coexist with moral failures?

  36. HOW: Retrieve Jdg 14:6, 19; 15:14 (Samson Spirit) vs 14:1-3; 16:1 (Philistine wife, prostitute); Jdg 6:34 (Gideon) vs 8:27 (ephod-idolatry); 1 Sam 10:10; 19:23-24 (Saul). Run concept_context.py on Jdg 11:29 scope=book. Conclusion test: is Spirit-empowerment a moral ratifier or a deliverance-enabler only?

  37. Heb 11:32-34 Greek aorists — what is actually commended.

    • WHAT: Map each aorist verb in Heb 11:33-34 to a specific faith-act in the commended figures' narratives; determine whether any aorist could refer to "sacrificed a child."
    • WHY: Henry's "honor-roll" argument. Faith-roll notoriously commends discrete acts (Samson's final prayer, David's lion-killing) not whole biographies (Samson's Philistine wife, David's adultery).
    • HOW: READ any Heb 11 existing materials. Run greek_parser.py --verse on Heb 11:32, 33, 34. Match each aorist (κατηγωνίσαντο, εἰργάσαντο, ἐπέτυχον, ἔφραξαν, ἔσβεσαν, ἔφυγον, ἐδυναμώθησαν, ἐγενήθησαν ἰσχυροί, ἔκλιναν) to the aligned Jephthah act — all are military-deliverance semantic fields mapping onto Jdg 11:32-33, not onto the vow. Verify Samson/David/Gideon have similar moral-mixed biographies — controls that Heb 11 does not endorse every act of every figure.

docs.title: External Corpus Leads (research directives)
docs.text:
1. Disjunctive-waw grammatical claim (Clarke 28187, Henry 9411). Verify: hebrew_parser + Waltke-O'Connor §39.2.1-3 via semantic_grammar.py. Parse ALL OT wehayah + X + we-QATAL vow/conditional constructions (Gen 28:20-22; Num 21:2; 1 Sam 1:11; 2 Sam 15:7-8) and tabulate whether any unambiguously carries disjunctive force. Scrutinize Exo 21:15 citation for noun-level vs clausal-verbal disjunction.

  2. LXX cross-check on Jdg 11:31, 39, 40 (v1 gap). Retrieve A-text and B-text for each verse. For 11:31: does LXX render we-haalitihu olahwith καί + ἀνοίσω ὁλοκαύτωμα (conjunctive) or ἤ (disjunctive)? For 11:39: what verb does LXX use forwayyaas lah et-nidro? For 11:40: is letannot rendered θρηνεῖν (lament) or διηγεῖσθαι/αἰνεῖν (recount/praise)? Compare with Jdg 5:11 yetannu → LXX.

  3. Clarke moral-impossibility argument (28216). Verify: retrieve Jdg 11:12-28 (Jephthah's diplomatic speech) with hebrew_parser; match against Num 20-21 and Deut 2:4-6, 9, 19 for textual dependency. Score Torah-literacy: does Jephthah demonstrably cite Pentateuch accurately? If yes, the "ignorant outlaw" defense collapses — which either (a) worsens the literal-sacrifice reading (literate Torah-violator) or (b) supports the dedication reading (literate + would choose Lev 27:1-8 redemption).

  4. Targum Jonathan / Phinehas-redemption gloss (Clarke 28231). Verify: retrieve Lev 27:1-8 Hebrew parse; confirm female 5-20 yrs = 10 shekels, 20-60 yrs = 30 shekels. Confirm Phinehas alive in Jephthah's era: retrieve Jdg 20:27-28 (Phinehas at Ark in immediately-preceding Benjamin war — chronological proximity). GENERATE /word-study H6187 erek. The Targum gloss frames Jephthah's fault as priestly-consultation failure, not vow-utterance or execution — a distinct thesis worth surfacing in analysis.

  5. Spirit-empowerment argument (Clarke 28230). Verify: run Jdg-internal controls — Samson Jdg 14:6, 19; 15:14 vs 14:1-3, 16:1; Gideon 6:34 vs 8:27; Saul 1 Sam 10:10 vs 19:23-24. Does the Judges author treat Spirit as moral ratifier or as deliverance-enabler? concept_context.py scope=book on Jdg 11:29.

  6. letannot verb-choice (Edersheim BHOTV3 140.1, Clarke 28288, Henry 9414). Verify: hebrew_parser lemma תנה (H8567); LXX Jdg 5:11 vs 11:40 verb-choice (as in directive 2 above); translation history KJV "lament" vs NASB/NET "commemorate/celebrate". Edersheim and Clarke both agree "praise" — Edersheim reads LITERAL sacrifice AND celebrative rite (which is internally odd if literal).

  7. bethulim + Henry's "no celibacy religion" counter (Henry 9412; Clarke 28288). Verify: retrieve ALL bethulim (H1331) occurrences with hebrew_parser. Test Henry's counter: is there Mosaic/priestly category of vowed-celibacy? Retrieve Exo 38:8, 1 Sam 2:22 (women serving at tent-door); Luk 2:36-37 (Anna, widow-prophetess dedicated to temple); 1 Cor 7:34 (unmarried-vowed). If living-vowed-virginity lacks clear Mosaic precedent, Henry's structural objection stands; v1's dedication reading then depends more heavily on the lexical and syntactic arguments.

  8. Josephus Ant. 5.7.6, 5.7.10 — literal sacrifice + Torah-violation + "living creature" paraphrase. Verify: This is first-century Jewish reception. The first datum (Ant. 5.7.6) is evidence for literal reading; the second (Ant. 5.7.10) is evidence that Josephus understood the vow as anticipating any living creature — supporting disjunctive-vow reading grammatically. Critically: Josephus declaring the act "neither conformable nor acceptable" neutralizes any "Heb 11:32 validates the vow" inference regardless of reading.

  9. Heb 11:32 honor-roll argument (Henry 9414). Verify directive #10 above: Greek parsing of aorists + matching to Jephthah's military victory (11:32-33), not the vow. Samson/David/Gideon as moral-mixed controls.

  10. Edersheim Torah-literacy argument (BHOTV3 141.1). Combined with Clarke's moral-impossibility (lead 3): if Jephthah is Torah-literate (Jdg 11:15-27), the literal-sacrifice reading makes him a knowing-violator (worse theologically) AND the dedication reading makes him a prudent redeemer (better theologically). This weighs against the "rough-times ignorance" excuse historically offered for the literal reading.

  11. Sirach 18:22 intertestamental vow-ethic. Verify: Eccl 5:4-6 is canonical control. Note that Torah's own Lev 5:4-5 off-ramp existed; Second Temple vow-absolutism may have colored Josephus' reading. Not a Hebrew-text argument, but a reception-history contextualization.

  12. Absence-of-community-override observation. Verify: retrieve 1 Sam 14:24-45 — narrative pattern (rash oath → people override → YHWH's will revealed). In Jdg 11, no override; annual four-day custom commemorates. Is this (a) communal moral collapse (sacrifice reading) or (b) no crisis to override, publicly-known honored dedication (dedication reading)?

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
grammar-analysis (heavy Hebrew-grammar component; vow-syntax is central; WX-QATAL, weqatal-apodosis, and disjunctive-waw are the hinge questions). Secondary: answer-question for the overall biblical-theology synthesis.

Scoped: 2026-04-17 Folder: bible-studies/jephthah-daughter-sacrifice/

docs.title: The Question
docs.text:
Judges 11 tells the story of Jephthah, a judge of Israel who made a vow before battle: whatever came out of the doors of his house to meet him on his return, he would offer as a burnt offering to the Lord. He won the battle -- and his only daughter, his only child, was the first one out to meet him.

Did Jephthah actually kill his daughter as a burnt offering? Or did he, as some interpreters have argued, dedicate her to a lifetime of virginity and temple service instead?

docs.title: The Short Answer
docs.text:
The biblical evidence points with high confidence to a literal human sacrifice. Jephthah made a rash battlefield vow using the standard Hebrew words for animal sacrifice. When his daughter came out, he responded with the Hebrew gestures of mourning for the dead (tearing his clothes, saying "I cannot go back"). She asked for two months to mourn her unmarried state, and when she returned, the text says he "did to her according to his vow."

It was a sinful, tragic act -- forbidden by Torah (Deuteronomy 12:31, Jeremiah 7:31), not commanded by God, and not approved by God. But Jephthah carried it through.

docs.title: Why Do Some People Think He Just Dedicated Her?
docs.text:
The "dedication" reading has a long history. It comes from several arguments:

1. The Hebrew word "and" (waw) could be read as "or." Some translators have proposed that the vow says "shall be the LORD's, OR I will offer it up" -- meaning a human would be dedicated, an animal would be sacrificed.

2. The daughter mourned her virginity, not her death. She wept over her "bethulim" (virginity) for two months on the mountains, not over her impending death.

3. After the vow was fulfilled, the text says "she knew no man." Some read this as meaning she lived the rest of her life as a virgin.

4. The narrator never explicitly says she was killed. The fulfillment verse just says "he did to her according to his vow."

5. Hebrews 11:32 praises Jephthah as a man of faith.

These arguments have been held by respected interpreters (Adam Clarke, David Kimchi, others). But on closer examination, each one collapses.

docs.title: Why the "Dedication" Reading Fails
docs.text:
1. The Hebrew "and" is not "or."

Hebrew has a dedicated word for "or" (o). The author of Judges uses it three verses later in the same passage (11:34, "neither son OR daughter"). He knew the word. He did not use it in the vow.

The ancient Greek translation (the Septuagint, used by Jews centuries before Christ) also did not read the waw as disjunctive. Both major manuscript traditions of the Septuagint render it with "and" or with no connective at all -- never with the Greek word for "or." Ancient Jewish readers did not see a disjunction here.

2. The vow uses the standard vocabulary for animal sacrifice -- not dedication.

Hebrew has at least five different vocabularies for putting a person under a lifelong God-ward obligation:

  • The dedication vocabulary ("give to the LORD all the days of his life" -- what Hannah said about Samuel)
  • The redemption vocabulary (Leviticus 27:1-8 lets anyone who vows a person redeem them with silver -- Torah's built-in off-ramp)
  • The consecration vocabulary (what God commanded for the firstborn)
  • The Nazirite vocabulary (Samson, Samuel, John the Baptist)
  • The ban vocabulary (cherem, for enemies in holy war)

Jephthah used none of them. He used the sacrificial vocabulary: "cause to go up" (Hiphil of alah) + "burnt offering" (olah). This exact combination, in every other Old Testament occurrence, means a literal burnt offering -- Noah after the flood, Abraham with Isaac on Mount Moriah, the Moabite king burning his son on the wall, Solomon's thousand sacrifices. There is not one figurative use.

3. "She knew no man" describes her state at that moment, not her future life.

This is a Hebrew grammatical construction called WX-QATAL -- a subject-fronted verb clause that describes background or attendant circumstance, not sequential action. Every parallel in the Old Testament describes a state at a specific moment, not a lifetime:

  • Genesis 3:1 -- "the serpent was crafty" (at the moment of the story)
  • Genesis 24:16 -- "Rebekah was a virgin and no man had known her" (when she came to the well -- she married within days)
  • 1 Kings 1:4 -- "the king knew her not" (at the moment Abishag was brought to him)
  • Judges 14:4 -- "his father and mother did not know" (at that specific moment)

The narrator is telling us that at the moment the vow was fulfilled, she was still a virgin. This makes sense of her two months of mourning her virginity beforehand -- she was weeping that she would die without ever marrying.

4. Jephthah's reaction is the Hebrew reaction to a death, not a sanctuary commitment.

When he saw her, he tore his clothes -- the standard Hebrew gesture for grief over death (Jacob for Joseph, David for Saul and Jonathan, Job). He said "I cannot go back." This is the language of an inescapable tragedy, not a bittersweet lifetime commitment.

5. "He did to her according to his vow" is compressed reference, not a denial.

The narrator says Jephthah "did to her his vow which he had vowed." The vow was stated explicitly in verse 31: "I will offer it up for a burnt offering." Telling us he carried out his stated vow does not soften the vow's content. It points back to it.

6. The annual four-day rite is a lament, not a commemoration of a living person.

The Hebrew word letannot (Judges 11:40) means to lament or rehearse in grief. The daughters of Israel went out yearly to grieve her. You don't yearly lament someone who is alive and serving in the sanctuary.

7. Hebrews 11:32 praises Jephthah's faith in battle, not his vow.

Hebrews 11:32-34 names Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, and Samuel together. The subsequent verses describe what they did "through faith": "subdued kingdoms, wrought righteousness, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions, quenched the violence of fire, escaped the edge of the sword, out of weakness were made strong, waxed valiant in fight, turned to flight the armies of the aliens." These are battlefield faith-acts. God's commendation of a man's faith in one arena does not ratify every subsequent decision he made. Samson is in the same list, and the Bible does not thereby endorse Samson's relationship with Delilah.

docs.title: Why Did God Let the Spirit of the Lord Come on Jephthah?
docs.text:
Judges 11:29 says "the Spirit of the LORD came upon Jephthah" before the vow. Doesn't that mean God approved what followed?

The Spirit in Judges is a deliverance-enabler, not a moral endorser. The same formula appears over Samson (Judges 14:6, 14:19, 15:14) -- and is immediately followed by Samson violating his Nazirite vow, visiting a prostitute, and making disastrous marriage choices. The Spirit's presence empowered deliverance. It did not sanctify every subsequent act.

Jephthah's vow comes after the Spirit narrative (v.29) and seems to be Jephthah's own invention -- a faithless bargaining with God for victory he already had the Spirit to accomplish. The Spirit was given for Israel's deliverance from Ammon. The vow was Jephthah's own rash addition.

docs.title: What About the Torah Off-Ramps?
docs.text:
This is one of the saddest parts of the story. Torah provided at least two clear ways out:

Leviticus 5:4-13 -- If anyone swears rashly with his lips, when he realizes the guilt, he brings a trespass offering and is forgiven. Jephthah could have confessed his rash vow, offered a sin offering, and the matter would have ended there.

Leviticus 27:1-8 -- When a person is vowed to the LORD, the priest assigns a silver valuation based on age and sex. An adult woman between twenty and sixty is valued at thirty shekels of silver. The vower pays the priest, and the person is redeemed.

The priest of the era was Phinehas, who is placed at the Ark in the same general period (Judges 20:27-28). He was alive and ministering. Jephthah could have gone to him.

He did not. The vow was fulfilled as stated.

docs.title: So What Is the Bible Teaching Us?
docs.text:
First, the Bible does not endorse everything it reports. The book of Judges is a book about spiritual decline. Its refrain is "in those days there was no king in Israel: every man did that which was right in his own eyes" (17:6, 21:25). Jephthah's story fits the pattern: a deliverer raised up by God accomplishes a real deliverance but also commits a grievous sin, because Israel has drifted far from the Torah. The narrative reports; it does not approve.

Second, rash vows are dangerous. Ecclesiastes 5:4-6 and Matthew 5:33-37 both warn against rash oaths. Jephthah's story is the clearest Old Testament object lesson for why.

Third, even genuinely called servants of God can fall into tragic sin. Jephthah was anointed by the Spirit, was a military deliverer, is named in Hebrews 11. And he killed his own daughter. This is a sobering witness that spiritual gifts, military success, and even faith in battle do not automatically produce moral wisdom in every other area of life.

Fourth, the Torah itself forbade what he did. Deuteronomy 12:31 says explicitly that burning sons and daughters is "every abomination to the LORD, which he hateth." Jeremiah 7:31 says of child sacrifice: "which I commanded them not, neither came it into my heart." Jephthah was not obeying God. He was violating God's own explicit command, driven by a rash vow and perhaps by a theology he had absorbed from the surrounding Canaanite cultures (Judges 11:24 shows he had some familiarity with Chemosh theology).

docs.title: An Honest Concession
docs.text:
The strongest remaining argument for the dedication reading is the narrator's restraint at 11:39. He does not say "he slew her" or "he offered her up." He says "he did to her his vow." Some readers feel that a narrator willing to say Mesha burned his son on the wall (2 Kings 3:27) or that Ahaz burned his son (2 Kings 16:3) would have been willing to say it plainly here.

But the narrator's restraint may itself be purposeful. The text has just described two months of a daughter's tears, a father's breaking voice, and an unbreakable vow. To narrate the killing in clinical detail would be to destroy the very restraint the text has established. The compressed "he did to her his vow" preserves the horror while letting the reader fill in what verse 31 already promised.

The Hebrew letannot (11:40) -- a verb that appears only here and in Judges 5:11 in the entire Old Testament -- also remains somewhat ambiguous. But the weight of the other evidence is strong enough to overcome these residual difficulties.

docs.title: The Bottom Line
docs.text:
Jephthah literally sacrificed his daughter as a burnt offering. It was sinful, forbidden by Torah, and never endorsed by God. The vow was rash; the fulfillment was tragic; the daughters of Israel wept for her every year.

The Bible records the event to show the depth of Israel's spiritual decline in the period of the judges -- and to warn every reader since against rash oaths made in moments of fear or bargaining.

Read the Full Conclusion Read the Full Analysis

docs.title: Concept Context
docs.text:
================================================================ CONCEPT-CONTEXT ANALYSIS Source: concept_context.py ================================================================

=== Jdg 11:31 === Concepts in verse: EXODUS: yatsa (H3318) — "that, cometh forth" [x3 tagging] REPENTANCE: shub (H7725) — "when I return" SACRIFICE: zebach/olah concept — H5930 ("for a burnt offering")

Same chapter matches (Jdg 11): 11:3 (EXODUS), 11:8 (REPENTANCE), 11:9 (REPENTANCE), 11:13 (REPENTANCE), 11:34 (EXODUS — daughter coming out), 11:35 (REPENTANCE — "I cannot go back"), 11:36 (EXODUS — "proceeded out of thy mouth"), 11:39 (REPENTANCE — "she returned").

Same book matches (top 10): Jdg 6:18 — Gideon asks angel to stay while he brings an offering Jdg 13:16 — angel of YHWH: "if thou wilt offer a burnt offering, offer it unto the LORD" (angel REFUSES self-sacrifice; commends sacrifice OF ANIMAL TO YHWH) Jdg 13:19 — Manoah's kid-with-meat-offering Jdg 13:23 — Manoah's wife: "If the LORD were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering..." [inference: YHWH's acceptance of sacrifice = life-affirming, not death- demanding] Jdg 15:19 — Samson revived by water

These nearby-book concepts cluster around SACRIFICE + DELIVERANCE motifs. Jdg 13:16-23 is especially instructive: the angel of YHWH does NOT permit personal-sacrifice and redirects to animal burnt-offering — the exact opposite of what a literal Jdg 11:39 would require.

=== Jdg 11:39 === Concepts in verse: TELOS: qets (H7093) — "at the end" REPENTANCE: shub (H7725) — "she returned" FULFILLMENT: asah (H6213) — "who did" (neutral verb, unlike olah/ zabach/saraph/ha'evir ba'esh) LAW: choq (H2706) — "a custom/statute"

Same chapter matches: 11:10 (FULFILLMENT — elders' pledge to Jephthah), 11:13, 11:27 ("the LORD the Judge"), 11:31, 11:35, 11:36.

Same book matches: Jdg 17:3 — Micah's mother dedicates silver "wholly unto the LORD" then makes graven image [FULFILLMENT + REPENTANCE clustering with dedicated-vow-gone-irregular — structural parallel to Jephthah's Torah-irregular vow] Jdg 17:4 — idem Jdg 2:17 — apostasy refrain (LAW) Jdg 21:15 — Israel repented of Benjamin-vow (rash oath + national recovery) Jdg 21:23 — Benjamin-virgin recovery (Benjamin's inheritance preserved via a VIRGIN-acquisition procedure — same lexical field as Jdg 11 daughter) Jdg 7:17 — Gideon's "so shall ye do"

Same author (Historical): Neh 1:9, 9:26, 9:29 — covenant/return/commandment cluster 1 Chr 16:12 — "remember his marvellous works" [FULFILLMENT] Ezra 9:14 — "break thy commandments" [LAW + REPENTANCE]

=== Lev 27:2 === Concepts found: LIFE (nefesh H5315 — "the persons")

Same-book matches: only Lev 11 food-law verses tagged under LIFE (semantic-classifier gap — the priestly vow-law did not match other priestly contexts on concepts).

Same-author matches (Moses): Deut 10:12, 10:22, 11:13, 11:18 — all "heart and soul" covenant fidelity texts. The semantic-classifier associates the vow-of-persons regulation with whole-life devotion, not sacrificial death.

Other books: 1 Chr 11:19 — David's rejection of blood-of-men offering ("shall I drink the blood of these men that have put their lives in jeopardy?") — STRONG conceptual parallel: David recoils from symbolic- drinking of life-endangered men's sacrifice. Also 1 Chr 22:19 — "set your heart and soul to seek the LORD" + 1 Chr 28:9 + 2 Chr 17:16 (Zichri's self-consecration). The Chronicler's theology = person- dedication is SOUL-SERVICE, not death-offering.

=== 1 Sam 1:11 (Hannah) === Concepts in verse: PROPHET: ra'ah/naba (semantic field) — "thou wilt, look, indeed" LIFE: chay (H2416) — "of his life" [kol yemei CHAYYAV]

Same-chapter: 1 Sam 1:10 (LIFE — "bitterness of soul"), 1:15 ("poured out my soul"), 1:22 ("abide for ever"), 1:26 ("thy soul liveth"). ALL LIFE- related. Hannah's vow-frame is entirely in the LIFE register, not the SACRIFICE register.

Same-book: 1 Sam 10:24 Samuel anoints Saul; 1 Sam 17:55 David chosen; 1 Sam 19:5 David's "life in hand" Philistine victory; 1 Sam 23:15 David pursued.

Same-author (Historical): 1 Kgs 1:34 Zadok/Nathan anointing; 1 Kgs 17:23 Elijah raises widow's son ("thy son liveth"); 1 Kgs 18:15, 19:10 ("LORD of hosts liveth"). The book's vocabulary of dedication-to-YHWH is paired with LIVING-service, never with burnt-offering immolation.

================================================================ KEY SEMANTIC-CLASSIFIER OBSERVATION ================================================================

The concept_context.py classifier independently tagged: - Jdg 11:31 (vow) with SACRIFICE (because of olah) - Jdg 11:39 (fulfillment) with FULFILLMENT (neutral asah) — NOT SACRIFICE - 1 Sam 1:11 (Hannah) with LIFE (because of chay) - Lev 27:2 with LIFE (because of nefesh)

That the fulfillment-verse (Jdg 11:39) sheds the SACRIFICE tag that the vow-verse (Jdg 11:31) carries is grammatically and lexically significant. The narrator's verb-choice (עשׂה asah vs. עלה Hiphil + olah) registers on automatic semantic classifiers.

docs.title: Greek Parsing (Hebrews 11)
docs.text:
================================================================ GREEK PARSING — Hebrews 11:32-34 (Faith-roll aorists) Source: greek_parser.py (N1904 / Text-Fabric) ================================================================

=== Hebrews 11:32 === Καὶ τί ἔτι λέγω; ἐπιλείψει με γὰρ διηγούμενον ὁ χρόνος περὶ Γεδεών, Βαράκ, Σαμψών, Ἰεφθάε, Δαυείδ τε καὶ Σαμουὴλ καὶ τῶν προφητῶν,

Key word Lemma Strong Parsing λέγω λέγω G3004 V-PAS-1S "should I speak" pres.act.subj.1sg ἐπιλείψει ἐπιλείπω G1952 V-FAI-3S "will fail" διηγούμενον διηγέομαι G1334 V-PNP-ASM "recounting/telling" [pres.M/P.ptcp — COGNATE concept to LXX Jdg 5:11 where διηγέομαι renders Hebrew תנה — same verb the author of Heb 11 uses for "recounting" the heroes' deeds] Ἰεφθάε Ἰεφθάε G2422 N-PRI "Jephthah" (indeclinable)

NOTE: Heb 11:32 LISTS the names — Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, prophets — but provides NO specific commendation in this verse. Commendations come in the aorist verbs of vv. 33-34.

=== Hebrews 11:33 — First AORIST CLUSTER === οἳ διὰ πίστεως κατηγωνίσαντο βασιλείας, ἠργάσαντο δικαιοσύνην, ἐπέτυχον ἐπαγγελιῶν, ἔφραξαν στόματα λεόντων,

"who through faith [X-aorist-verb]..."

Verb Lemma Strong Parsing Semantic domain κατηγωνίσαντο καταγωνίζομαι G2610 V-ADI-3P conquered kingdoms (military victory) ἠργάσαντο ἐργάζομαι G2038 V-ADI-3P wrought righteousness (just rule) ἐπέτυχον ἐπιτυγχάνω G2013 V-2AAI-3P obtained promises (received oracle) ἔφραξαν φράσσω G5420 V-AAI-3P stopped mouths of lions (Daniel, Samson, David)

ALL four verbs = aorist indicative 3rd plural — describe DISCRETE acts of military-deliverance and administrative-righteousness, NOT priestly/cultic acts.

=== Hebrews 11:34 — Second AORIST CLUSTER === ἔσβεσαν δύναμιν πυρός, ἔφυγον στόματα μαχαίρης, ἐδυναμώθησαν ἀπὸ ἀσθενείας, ἐγενήθησαν ἰσχυροὶ ἐν πολέμῳ, παρεμβολὰς ἔκλιναν ἀλλοτρίων.

Verb Lemma Strong Parsing Semantic domain ἔσβεσαν σβέννυμι G4570 V-AAI-3P quenched violence of fire (Dan 3 furnace) ἔφυγον φεύγω G5343 V-2AAI-3P escaped sword's edge ἐδυναμώθησαν δυναμόω G1412 V-API-3P were strengthened from weakness ἐγενήθησαν ἰσχυροὶ γίνομαι+ἰσχυρός V-AOI-3P + A became mighty in war ἔκλιναν κλίνω G2827 V-AAI-3P turned to flight armies of aliens

ALL nine aorist-verbs in vv. 33-34 belong to the military-deliverance semantic field — armies defeated, promises received, mouths of lions shut, fire quenched, sword escaped, weakness strengthened, battle-valor gained, enemy camps turned.

NONE of the nine aorists describes a priestly/cultic act, a vow, a sacrifice, or any action specifically marriageable to Jdg 11:30-31 or 11:39 (the vow or its fulfillment). Every aorist maps naturally to Jdg 11:29 + 11:32-33 — Jephthah's SPIRIT-empowered military campaign against Ammon, 20 cities smitten, Ammon subdued.

CONTROLS (same principle elsewhere in Heb 11): - Samson — κατηγωνίσαντο + ἐδυναμώθησαν cover his lion, jawbone, and temple- pull-down; Heb 11 does NOT endorse his Philistine wife (Jdg 14:1-3) or his visit to the harlot (Jdg 16:1). - David — ἐργάσαντο δικαιοσύνην; Heb 11 does NOT endorse his Bathsheba/Uriah affair. - Gideon — κατηγωνίσαντο; Heb 11 does NOT endorse his gold-ephod idolatry (Jdg 8:27). - Barak — ἐπέτυχον ἐπαγγελιῶν (received the promise Deborah spoke).

THE FAITH-ROLL COMMENDS DISCRETE FAITH-ACTS, NOT WHOLE BIOGRAPHIES. Heb 11:32 is therefore SYMMETRICAL on the Jephthah-vow question — it commends his faith in the Ammonite campaign (ch. 11:29-33) without saying anything one way or the other about the vow. The vow is not among the nine aorists; the military deliverance is.

=== Greek Text Compare: N1904 vs TR (Heb 11:32) === N1904: Καὶ τί ἔτι λέγω; ἐπιλείψει με γὰρ διηγούμενον ὁ χρόνος περὶ Γεδεών, Βαράκ, Σαμψών, Ἰεφθάε, Δαυείδ τε καὶ Σαμουὴλ καὶ τῶν προφητῶν TR: και τι ετι 1952 γαρ 1334 ο περι γεδεων βαρακ και σαμψων και ιεφθαε δαβιδ τε και σαμουηλ και των [Textual variant detected — minor spelling/form variation, nothing affecting the name Ἰεφθάε or the structure of the list. Both texts include Jephthah.]

docs.title: Hebrew Parsing (Judges 11)
docs.text:
================================================================ HEBREW PARSING — Judges 11 Vow Narrative + Controls Source: hebrew_parser.py (ETCBC/BHSA via Text-Fabric) ================================================================

=== Judges 11:29 — Spirit of the LORD === וַתְּהִי עַל־יִפְתָּח רוּחַ יְהוָה וַיַּעֲבֹר אֶת־הַגִּלְעָד וְאֶת־מְנַשֶּׁה וַיַּעֲבֹר אֶת־מִצְפֵּה גִלְעָד וּמִמִּצְפֵּה גִלְעָד עָבַר בְּנֵי עַמּוֹן

KEY: וַתְּהִי (Qal.Wayq.3fs היה "be") + עַל־יִפְתָּח + רוּחַ יְהוָה KEY: wayyiqtol chain — עבר (pass over) × 3, the movement across Gilead/Manasseh

=== Judges 11:30 — Vow Formula === וַיִּדַּר יִפְתָּח נֶדֶר לַיהוָה וַיֹּאמַר אִם־נָתוֹן תִּתֵּן אֶת־בְּנֵי עַמּוֹן בְּיָדִי

Word Lemma Parsing Gloss וַיִּדַּר נדר Verb.Qal.Wayq.3ms vowed [cognate-accusative] יִפְתָּח יפתח Jephthah נֶדֶר נדר Noun.ms.Abs a vow לַיהוָה יהוה to YHWH וַיֹּאמַר אמר Verb.Qal.Wayq.3ms and said אִם אם Conj if נָתוֹן נתן Verb.Qal.InfAbs giving [emphatic inf.abs.] תִּתֵּן נתן Verb.Qal.Impf.2ms thou wilt give אֶת־בְּנֵי עַמּוֹן the sons of Ammon בְּיָדִי into my hand

STRUCTURE: vow-formula narrative frame (wayyiqtol × 2) + protasis head אִם + inf.abs.+impf.
